def _trash_clone(entry: Path, clone_root: Path):
    """Move an evicted auto-clone into ``.trash`` so it stays recoverable.

    Returns the new path on success, or ``None`` if the move was refused or the
    entry had to be deleted instead. A running profile is never moved (selection
    already excludes live sessions; this is belt-and-suspenders). If the rename
    fails (e.g. a Windows lock) the storage cap must still be honored, so we fall
    back to a best-effort delete — strictly no worse than the old behavior.
    """
    if _profile_has_running_browser(entry):
        return None
    trash = _clone_trash_dir(clone_root)
    try:
        trash.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    except OSError:
what: server.py (4207 lines, docstring 'Main MCP server for browser automation') embeds a full filesystem trash/quota/eviction subsystem directly alongside all 96 @mcp.tool registrations: _trash_clone, _purge_expired_trash, _idle_autoclones_over_cap, _enforce_clone_storage_cap_in, _session_storage_cap_bytes, _named_profiles_over_session_cap, _enforce_named_profile_trim_in, _enforce_session_storage, _run_storage_sweep, _copy_profile_tree, _rmtree_robust and further helpers spanning roughly lines 330-1080 — over 700 lines of clone-storage business logic living in what is nominally the tool-routing file.
why_it_matters: Any trace that touches an existing tool or adds a new one (2 of the 3 traced requests) means reading past hundreds of lines of unrelated clone-eviction filesystem logic to find the right MCP-tool code. It also means the 14 test files that `import server` directly (verified) exercise storage-sweep behavior, tool behavior, and profile-selection logic through the same single module import, so a change anywhere in the file risks rippling into unrelated test failures, and the storage-quota/trash system hardened across several recent commits (e5ced24, 83c7993, af4225b) sits one accidental edit away from any tool change.
fix_direction: Extract the profile/clone storage-sweep block (trash, purge, cap-enforcement, profile-copy helpers) into a dedicated clone_storage.py module with its own tests; leave server.py holding only tool registration, section-gating, and thin delegation calls into that module.
adv_rebuttal: Verified all 11 named storage/trash/eviction helpers are defined inside server.py, spanning L449-L888 (_trash_clone@449 ... _copy_profile_tree@888), confirming ~700 lines of clone-storage business logic embedded in the nominal tool-routing file. This is a distinct maintainability point (domain mixing / blast radius into the 14 import-server test files) from the god-object size finding. Holds.

``ResponseHandler.handle_response`` is synchronous and returns a plain
dict/list. ``await``-ing its return value raises ``TypeError: object dict
can't be used in 'await' expression`` at runtime — which silently broke the
``extract_element_assets``, ``extract_related_files`` and
``discover_object_methods`` tools: the CDP work completed, then the return
line threw on every call.
what: response_handler.handle_response() (response_handler.py:65) is a synchronous method, but it is called from inside async def MCP tool wrappers in server.py that otherwise await almost everything around them. Three call sites (extract_element_assets, extract_related_files, discover_object_methods) were written as `return await response_handler.handle_response(...)`, which raises TypeError on every invocation, and shipped that way until fixed in commit 2267b83 (the commit this audit is pinned at).
why_it_matters: This is exactly the class of bug the codebase's own tests cannot catch cheaply: server.py registers 96 tools and, per the fix commit's own test docstring, 'a live-browser test can't cover all 96 tools cheaply' — so the bug shipped silently, running real CDP work and then throwing on the return statement, on every call, for three shipped tools. The fix is a bespoke AST-walking static test (assert no ast.Await ever wraps a .handle_response attribute call) rather than a change that makes the mistake structurally impossible, so any other synchronous helper added to this heavily-async file in the future gets no equivalent protection unless someone remembers to write another bespoke AST guard for it.
fix_direction: Make handle_response a coroutine so await is always correct and the mismatch class disappears, or rename it (e.g. handle_response_sync) so the call convention is visually unambiguous at every call site; keep the existing AST guard as a regression test either way.
adv_rebuttal: The bug is already fixed at HEAD: zero `await response_handler.handle_response` calls remain, all sites now synchronous with explicit guard comments (L2844/L2916/L3700). The finding concedes it was fixed in the pinned commit; its only live substance is a critique of the AST guard - which actually IS a structural CI check that blocks that exact mistake repo-wide (it merely doesn't generalize to other sync helpers). A fixed defect plus a partly-wrong 'not structural' critique does not warrant High.

what: Five separate modules implement overlapping 'extract data from a DOM element' functionality with largely mirrored method sets: element_cloner.py (ElementCloner.extract_element_styles/_structure/_events/_animations/_assets, extract_related_files, clone_element_complete — verified via grep), file_based_element_cloner.py (the same seven operations, each suffixed _to_file), plus cdp_element_cloner.py, comprehensive_element_cloner.py, and progressive_element_cloner.py as further independent implementations — 2,223 lines total across the 5 files (verified via wc -l). file_based_element_cloner.py does not even fully delegate: it imports and wraps both ComprehensiveElementCloner and the element_cloner singleton directly, per the quoted import block, rather than one implementation being layered cleanly on another.
why_it_matters: Any change to what 'extracting an element' means (a new field to capture, a new fallback strategy, a bug fix in the querying logic) is a judgment call about which of 5 files need the same fix, with no compiler or test to catch a forgotten sibling. The MEDIUM trace in this audit shows the concrete cost: a new extraction-adjacent tool has 3 non-agreeing precedents to choose from for something as basic as 'where do I write the output file' (see F-205), because the cloner layer was never consolidated to one core.
fix_direction: Consolidate around one extraction core (comprehensive_element_cloner, since it is already imported by the file-based variant) with to_file/in-memory/progressive/CDP-only behavior expressed as thin adapters/strategies over it, not five parallel implementations.
adv_rebuttal: Verified 5 cloner modules with LOC 649/648/344/321/266 = 2228 (finding's 2223 differs only by trailing-newline counting), and file_based_element_cloner does wrap both ComprehensiveElementCloner and the element_cloner singleton rather than layering cleanly. Real duplication with no enforced sync. Note it is near-identical to F-601 and overlaps F-140; recommend main merge F-203/F-601.

what: DebugLogger is one global, module-level singleton (`debug_logger = DebugLogger()`, debug_logger.py:498) shared by every browser instance/session the singleton backend process is currently serving. log_error's de-duplication key is `component.method.exception_type.message` — component/method are fixed string tags like "browser_manager"/"spawn_browser", not the calling instance_id — so the first session to hit a given error shape suppresses every later occurrence from any other concurrent session for the rest of the process's life (bounded only by MAX_SEEN_ERRORS=1000 eventually clearing the whole set). All 287 verified debug_logger.* call sites across 15 files share this same no-instance-dimension shape.
why_it_matters: This directly blocks the traced cross-cutting request (per-session log correlation) at the root: there is no instance/session concept anywhere in the logging layer to build on, so adding one means touching the class plus, in the worst case, all 287 call sites. It is also a live operability gap today, independent of any new feature: an operator investigating one session's failure cannot filter to it, and a second session's genuinely-recurring error can be silently and permanently dropped because a different session already logged 'the same shaped' error first.
fix_direction: Thread instance_id through DebugLogger (optional param, included in the dedup key and stored on each entry), or adopt a contextvars.ContextVar set once per spawned browser task so call sites don't need individual edits; add an AST-level or runtime test asserting log/error entries carry a session dimension, analogous to test_server_call_conventions.py.
adv_rebuttal: Fact confirmed: one global debug_logger singleton (L498); the dedup key is f'{component}.{method}.{type(error).__name__}.{str(error)}' (L76) with NO instance_id anywhere in the module (verified absent), so the first session suppresses a same-shaped error for every other session until the set clears at 1000 (L82-83). But in a LOCAL single-user tool, cross-session log suppression is a low-impact operability gap, not High; the real weight is the 287-call-site refactor cost, which is a maintainability concern rather than a live High-severity defect.

what: The codebase has at least three independent 'write an artifact to disk' implementations that disagree: (1) response_handler.default_clone_output_dir() (response_handler.py:11) resolves a shared per-user directory (~/.stealth-mcp/element_clones, overridable via STEALTH_MCP_CLONE_OUTPUT_DIR) used automatically by ResponseHandler.handle_response()'s size-based fallback; (2) file_based_element_cloner.py reuses that same directory helper for its default but then hand-rolls its own _generate_filename/_save_to_file; (3) take_screenshot (quoted here) ignores both — with no file_path argument it writes to an OS tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile, and when the caller supplies file_path it writes to that exact path with zero validation against the per-user output root every other artifact-producing tool respects.
why_it_matters: There is no single blessed helper a new file-writing tool (like the MEDIUM trace's download_response_body) can call and be consistent with the rest of the server; a new author has to discover the disagreement by reading three files. take_screenshot's caller-supplied-path branch also means a client can already direct this MCP server to write a screenshot to an arbitrary filesystem path outside the documented per-user directory, undermining the guarantee response_handler.py's own docstring describes ('Must never resolve inside the installed package ... mirrors the project's existing state-dir convention').
fix_direction: Give every artifact-writing tool a single shared resolve_output_path(requested_path, default_subdir) helper (in response_handler.py or a new small module) that validates/anchors relative paths under default_clone_output_dir(), and make it the only thing take_screenshot, file_based_element_cloner, and any future file-writing tool call.

what: The singleton backend's decision to reuse a running backend process vs. start a fresh one is keyed on _server_version(), which reads the installed package's version metadata (importlib.metadata.version(SERVER_NAME)) — not a hash or mtime of the actual embedded/*.py source files. _start_backend_holding_lock (singleton.py:284) treats `_find_running_server() is not None` as '# already up (same version)' and returns without starting a new process.
why_it_matters: Every one of the 3 traced feature requests edits files inside embedded/ (server.py, browser_manager.py, debug_logger.py, etc.) without necessarily bumping pyproject.toml's version (currently 1.2.0). Because reuse is gated on that version string, a developer iterating locally — editing server.py, then manually invoking a tool to check the change — keeps talking to the pre-edit backend process indefinitely, with zero error or warning; only a manual backend kill or a version bump forces a fresh process. None of the 34 test files catch this because they `import server` in-process (verified), bypassing the singleton/proxy entirely, so the test suite gives no signal this failure mode exists. This is a recurring, previously-observed source of 'I fixed it but it's still broken' confusion during manual verification on this exact branch.
fix_direction: Key backend reuse on a content hash of the embedded/ package (e.g. hash of all .py files' mtimes+sizes, or a build-time-generated constant) in addition to or instead of the installed version string, so any source edit forces a fresh backend regardless of whether pyproject.toml was bumped.
adv_rebuttal: Confirmed: _find_running_server (singleton.py:117-136) reuses when `state.get('version') == _server_version()` (132), and _server_version (263-269) reads importlib.metadata — there is no source hash/mtime. The special-scrutiny question 'does anything invalidate on source change?' is answered no: this branch's version-aware work targets CROSS-version reuse (issue #14), not in-place same-version edits, and 14 test files import server in-process (bypassing the proxy). Matches the project's own saved memory note.

what: A browser instance's state is represented in parallel across at least four places: BrowserOptions (models.py, spawn-time config), BrowserManager's own live instance tracking, BrowserInstance (models.py, a pydantic subset of options + runtime state), and persistent_storage.InMemoryStorage.store_instance (called from browser_manager.py:480), which hand-builds a fifth, hardcoded-subset dict as quoted — instance_id/state/created_at/current_url/title/tabs only, silently dropping every other field (headless, user_agent, viewport, and any new option such as the SMALL trace's locale).
why_it_matters: Adding any new per-instance field (as in the traced SMALL request) requires a human to separately notice and decide whether persistent_storage needs the same field; today it silently does not track most of what BrowserInstance already has, and nothing fails when a new field is added and this layer is forgotten — it just continues serializing an incomplete, silently-stale snapshot that list_instances (server.py:1430) and shutdown-time logging both read from.
fix_direction: Have store_instance accept and serialize the actual BrowserInstance pydantic model (.model_dump()) instead of manually re-listing fields, so new fields propagate automatically.

what: BrowserManager.spawn_browser (browser_manager.py:311) is a single ~236-line method (the next method, _setup_dynamic_hooks, starts at line 548) that interleaves BrowserInstance construction, proxy config/forwarder startup, platform/executable detection, idle-timeout resolution, stealth-arg merging, uc.Config/uc.start() launch, CDP post-launch overrides (timezone), process-cleanup tracking, and diagnostics assembly, all in one function body under one top-level try/except. The quoted lines show proxy config, platform info, and idle-timeout resolution already adjacent within the first 10 lines of that block.
why_it_matters: This is the method every one of the 96 tools' most fundamental operation (creating a browser) runs through, and it is also where the traced SMALL request (a new spawn-time option) must be inserted. There is no natural seam — 'post-launch CDP overrides' is not its own step, just adjacent statements — so adding behavior safely requires reading nearly the whole method to understand ordering and side-effect dependencies (e.g. proxy_forwarder must start before uc.Config is built; timezone override must run after uc.start() returns a live tab). It cannot be unit-tested in isolation without mocking most of nodriver; the only real coverage is the integration-marked, real-Chrome-required suite.
fix_direction: Split into an explicit pipeline of small private methods (resolve_proxy, resolve_launch_args, launch_browser, apply_post_launch_overrides, track_process) called in sequence from a short orchestrating spawn_browser, each independently unit-testable with a fake tab/browser.

what: server.py is 4207 lines with 162 top-level function/async-def definitions, of which 96 are MCP tools registered via this single @section_tool() decorator across 11 'sections' (browser-management, element-interaction, element-extraction, file-extraction, network-debugging, cdp-functions, progressive-cloning, cookies-storage, tabs, debugging, dynamic-hooks). The decorator is the only indirection layer that exists — every tool's full implementation (signature, docstring, business logic, error handling) is written inline in this one file. There is no per-domain module split (no tabs.py, no cookies.py, etc.); DOMHandler/NetworkInterceptor/ResponseHandler are the only pieces factored out, and even those are consumed via bare module-level singletons imported at the top of this same file. Adding one tool means editing server.py regardless of which of the 11 sections it belongs to. Because the whole file is one Python module, a single import-time failure anywhere (a bad decorator argument, a broken helper, a bad top-level statement) prevents ALL 96 tools across all 11 sections from registering — the SECTION_TOOLS/DISABLED_SECTIONS mechanism (server.py:50-51, 1220-1227) only prunes tools AFTER a successful full-file import via apply_disabled_sections(), so it provides zero fault isolation between sections at load time despite looking like a section boundary.
why_it_matters: This is the single highest-leverage maintainability problem in the codebase: every contributor touches the same 4207-line file for any tool-level change, inflating review diffs and merge-conflict risk, and the file's apparent section boundaries are cosmetic for reliability purposes — the 11 'sections' share one failure domain despite being independently toggle-able at runtime via --disable-<section> flags.
fix_direction: Split server.py into one module per section (e.g. tools/browser_management.py, tools/cdp_functions.py, ...), each registering its own tools against the shared mcp instance via the existing section_tool decorator (or an equivalent registry). Reduce server.py to a composition root: singleton construction, lifespan, argument parsing, and importing the per-section modules. This also buys real fault isolation — a broken cdp-functions module then fails only that import, not the other 10 sections.
adv_rebuttal: Verified exact: server.py is 4208 lines, 162 top-level defs, 96 @section_tool decorators across 11 distinct sections, SECTION_TOOLS@51 / DISABLED_SECTIONS@50, apply_disabled_sections@1220 - every cited number matches. The 'one import unit = shared failure domain' claim is structurally true: any import-time error aborts all 96 registrations before apply_disabled_sections can prune, so sections give zero load-time isolation. No factual error; it is the genuine top maintainability issue. Note: F-505 and F-612 are duplicates of this same root cause.

what: hot_reload() (server.py:2975) calls importlib.reload(sys.modules['browser_manager']) then does browser_manager = BrowserManager(). But the name BrowserManager in server.py's own namespace was bound once, at import time, via 'from browser_manager import BrowserManager' (server.py:26); importlib.reload() re-executes browser_manager.py's code inside its own existing module object, updating sys.modules['browser_manager'].BrowserManager, but it cannot reach back into server.py's namespace to update the separately-copied name BrowserManager that the 'from...import' statement created. The 'global browser_manager, BrowserManager' statement only grants permission to assign; the function body never actually reassigns BrowserManager itself. So every hot_reload() call instantiates the original, pre-reload BrowserManager class, silently. The identical pattern repeats for NetworkInterceptor and DOMHandler a few lines later. The function unconditionally returns "Hot reload completed. Reloaded modules: ..." (server.py:3009), reporting success while not actually picking up any class-level code changes. Separately, hot_reload's module list ('browser_manager', 'network_interceptor', 'dom_handler', 'debug_logger', 'models') never includes server.py itself, so none of the 162 functions / 96 tools defined in server.py — the vast majority of the code — can be hot-reloaded by this mechanism under any circumstance.
why_it_matters: This directly explains a known operability pain point: applying server.py edits live requires killing and restarting the backend process rather than trusting hot_reload or a client reconnect. The tool exists, looks like it should work, and actively reports success, but structurally cannot refresh the class definitions it claims to reload. A developer trusting its 'completed' message is silently exercising stale code — worse than having no hot-reload at all, because it hides the need to restart.
fix_direction: Either delete hot_reload as misleading, or fix it properly: after importlib.reload, re-fetch each class via getattr(sys.modules[module_name], 'BrowserManager') and rebind server.py's own BrowserManager/NetworkInterceptor/DOMHandler names too (not just the lowercase instance names), and document plainly that it can never reload server.py's own tool bodies — for that, a full process restart remains unavoidable in the current single-file architecture.
adv_rebuttal: Confirmed: server.py:26 binds `BrowserManager` via `from browser_manager import`, and hot_reload line 2997 calls the bare `BrowserManager()`, so importlib.reload (2993) re-executes the module but cannot rebind server.py's own global name — it instantiates the stale, pre-reload class. Reload list (2983-2989) also excludes server.py itself. My best refutation (that it re-imports the class the way it re-imports debug_logger at 3006) fails: only debug_logger is re-imported; BrowserManager/NetworkInterceptor/DOMHandler are not (2996-3003).

what: server.py mutates process-wide state as a side effect of being imported, with no single init/teardown boundary. Three instances: (1) four singleton objects — browser_manager, network_interceptor, dom_handler, cdp_function_executor — are constructed at bare module scope (server.py:1297-1300) purely by importing the file; (2) 'if parse_bool_env("XPOOL_SAFE_MODE", default=False): DISABLED_SECTIONS.add("cdp-functions"); apply_disabled_sections()' runs at module scope (server.py:4072-4074), so importing server.py can silently unregister a whole tool section depending on an environment variable, independent of the if __name__ == "__main__" CLI path; (3) _install_nodriver_cookie_compat() (quoted above, server.py:195-213) monkey-patches the third-party nodriver library's cdp_network.Cookie.from_json static method in place, rewriting how a dependency parses CDP payloads for the whole process, guarded only by a marker attribute set on the library's own class object.
why_it_matters: None of this lives behind an explicit init()/configure() call — it all fires from module import. That's why tests/test_browser_integration.py resorts to manual importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(...) plus a bare try/except around exec_module just to import server safely, instead of a plain 'import server'. The running server's behavior (which tool sections are live, which library methods are patched) depends on environment variables read once at import time, which is easy to get out of sync between environments and hard to reason about from any single function in isolation.
fix_direction: Move singleton construction and the XPOOL_SAFE_MODE gate into app_lifespan or an explicit create_app() factory instead of module scope. Keep the nodriver monkey-patch (it already has an idempotency marker, so it's safe to call once) but invoke it explicitly from that same factory rather than relying on import order.

what: 22 separate except-Exception/bare-except blocks in server.py (verified count) each hand-roll their own error shape; at least 4 distinct, mutually incompatible contracts coexist. (a) execute_script (quoted above, ~1939-1949) returns {"success": bool, "result": ..., "error": ...}. (b) get_debug_lock_status (server.py:2549, except at 2558) returns a bare {"error": str(e)} with no "success" key at all. (c) new_tab (server.py:2636, except at 2662) does 'raise Exception(f"Failed to create new tab: {str(e)}")' — a second, generic, re-wrapped exception that discards the original exception type. (d) hot_reload (server.py:2975) and reload_status (server.py:3013) both declare '-> str' and return a plain string on EITHER success or failure ("Hot reload completed..." vs "Hot reload failed: ...") — callers cannot distinguish success from failure by type at all, only by string-matching the text. The one shared helper, _with_cdp_timeout (server.py:139), also just does 'raise Exception(...)' on timeout rather than a typed/structured error.
why_it_matters: With 96 tools and no shared error envelope or exception hierarchy, every caller (including the AI agent driving this MCP server) must special-case each tool's failure shape individually, and there is no single place to fix or extend error reporting (error codes, retryability hints, etc.) without touching all 96 call sites. No test in the suite checks error-shape consistency, so nothing stops this from getting worse as new tools are added.
fix_direction: Introduce one small ok()/err() helper (or adopt FastMCP's structured tool-error mechanism if available) and route all 96 except-blocks through it. A follow-up static check, in the same style as tests/test_server_call_conventions.py, can then assert no tool hand-builds its own error dict/string.
adv_rebuttal: Confirmed all four incompatible shapes at the cited lines: execute_script returns {success,error} (L1939-1948), get_debug_lock_status returns bare {'error':str(e)} with no success key (L2559), new_tab re-raises a generic Exception (L2663), hot_reload/reload_status are '-> str' on both success and failure (L2975/L3013), and _with_cdp_timeout raises a plain Exception (L139). The 22-block / 4-contract inconsistency holds and is a real maintainability burden for the AI caller. Cannot refute.

quote:
"""Static call-convention guards for embedded/server.py.

``ResponseHandler.handle_response`` is synchronous and returns a plain
dict/list. ``await``-ing its return value raises ``TypeError: object dict
can't be used in 'await' expression`` at runtime — which silently broke the
``extract_element_assets``, ``extract_related_files`` and
``discover_object_methods`` tools: the CDP work completed, then the return
line threw on every call.

A live-browser test can't cover all 96 tools cheaply, but the convention is
statically checkable: parse server.py and assert no ``await`` ever wraps a
``handle_response`` call. This fails on any reintroduction, regardless of
which tool it lands in.
what: None of the 96 @section_tool-decorated coroutine bodies in server.py have direct unit tests. The only coverage is (a) tests/test_browser_integration.py, marked pytest.mark.integration, which requires a real installed Chrome and is skippable/skipped in CI without Chrome, or (b) narrow AST-level static conventions like the quoted file. This gap already caused a real, silent production bug fixed on the current commit (2267b83, "fix: don't await synchronous handle_response at 3 extraction call sites", touching server.py:2845-2963 area) — three tools raised TypeError on every single call, undetected until runtime, because nothing exercised those handler bodies directly. Pure-logic helpers that don't touch a browser (e.g. _resolve_profile_selection and friends) DO get fast unit tests (tests/test_profile_resolution.py) by being extracted to module scope as plain functions — but that pattern doesn't extend to the 96 tool bodies themselves, which mix request handling, delegation, and response shaping inline inside the @section_tool coroutines.
why_it_matters: The new AST-based regression test is a good reactive guard for the one specific mistake it checks, but it's a symptom patch, not a fix for the underlying gap: any other tool-body mistake (wrong argument order, wrong handler call, wrong response shape) has the same blind spot until either a human runs the affected tool by hand or the skippable integration suite happens to cover it.
fix_direction: Extract each tool's request-validation/response-shaping logic into a plain function that takes already-fetched data (not a live tab) and returns the response dict, so it's unit-testable with fakes/mocks; keep the @section_tool coroutine itself as a thin adapter that fetches the tab and calls that function — this mirrors the pattern already proven for profile resolution.
adv_rebuttal: Accurate: the 96 @section_tool coroutine bodies have no direct unit tests; only skippable integration tests (need Chrome) and the AST convention test touch them. The gap is partly inherent to browser-driving code, but it is real and the F-202 TypeError bug is the proof it bites. Could not find any non-integration test that imports and calls a tool body.

Unlike F-105's gap, this function IS unit tested directly (tests/test_profile_resolution.py, no browser needed, tmp_path + env-var patches), so regressions are caught — but any change still requires holding the whole branching tree plus the 3-function retry protocol in your head simultaneously, since a role string, a source path, and a side-effecting sweep trigger are all threaded through by convention rather than by type.
fix_direction: Split into a pure _decide_profile_source(...) (branching only, no I/O, returns a small dataclass with the chosen role/source/kind) and a separate _materialize_profile(...) that performs the filesystem side effects — makes the decision tree trivially testable in isolation from disk I/O and shrinks the retry-coordination surface.

what: create_python_binding (server.py:3808) executes caller-supplied python_code via bare exec(python_code, exec_globals) with an empty globals dict — which still has full access to __builtins__ (Python auto-injects it), so there is no restriction on imports, file access, or process control. exec_globals is then scanned for the first non-underscore callable and wired up as a live Python<->JS binding. This is one of two tools that can run arbitrary Python in the server process; the sibling execute_python_in_browser (server.py:3827) delegates to cdp_function_executor.execute_python_in_browser (a Python-to-JS translator, not audited here per the coupling-only scope for that module) rather than exec'ing directly, so create_python_binding is the more direct instance of the pattern.
why_it_matters: Any caller of this MCP tool — including a compromised or confused upstream AI agent, or any process able to reach this server's transport — gets unrestricted code execution with the server process's full privileges (filesystem, network, environment variables, other browser instances' profile data). This is presumably intentional given the product's purpose (AI-driven browser automation with a Python-JS bridge), but it maximizes blast radius: nothing else in server.py needs to fail for this single tool to be a full process-compromise vector if it is ever reachable by untrusted input.
fix_direction: If this capability must stay, at minimum restrict exec_globals's __builtins__ to a small explicit allow-list, and document the trust boundary (this tool assumes a trusted caller) directly in the tool's docstring/description so it is an explicit, reviewed decision rather than an incidental one.
adv_rebuttal: The code is real: exec_globals={} then exec(python_code, exec_globals) at server.py:3847-3848, with __builtins__ auto-injected. But python_code is strictly the MCP tool-call parameter (signature L3830) - always operator/AI-authored, never page- or network-derived - so in this LOCAL single-user tool it is self-inflicted, not RCE. The finding itself concedes it is 'presumably intentional given the product's purpose.' Downgrade the security framing; residual is a minor robustness note.

quote: parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Stealth Browser MCP Server with 90 tools")
what: Three different hand-maintained numbers describe 'how many tools' this server exposes, and none is derived from the others: the CLI description says '90 tools' (server.py:4085, quoted); --list-sections's printed per-section breakdown (server.py, inside the args.list_sections branch) sums to 99 (11+8+10+9+10+15+10+3+5+6+12 across the 11 listed sections); the actual count of @section_tool( decorators in the file is 96 (verified via grep -c). All three would agree automatically if derived from SECTION_TOOLS (e.g. len(SECTION_TOOLS) sections, sum(len(v) for v in SECTION_TOOLS.values()) tools) instead of being typed by hand in three separate places.
why_it_matters: Low-stakes by itself, but it's a concrete, easily-verified symptom of the file being too large for its own internal bookkeeping to stay consistent — a proxy for the same god-object problem described in F-101, at the granularity of 'nobody can even keep the tool count straight.'
fix_direction: Compute both strings from SECTION_TOOLS at runtime instead of hardcoding three separate numbers.

what: server.py calls leading-underscore ('private') members of collaborator modules it doesn't own instead of their public API, in 3 places: process_cleanup._cleanup_all_tracked() (server.py:1261, in app_lifespan's shutdown path, quoted above), getattr(process_cleanup, "_get_browser_pids_for_profile", None) (server.py:272), and debug_logger._enabled (server.py:4141, in the CLI entrypoint's --debug handling).
why_it_matters: Small in count but each is an implicit contract with a module that never advertised that attribute as stable, so a rename inside process_cleanup.py or debug_logger.py silently breaks server.py with no import-time signal — the opposite of the explicit-public-API delegation pattern server.py otherwise uses cleanly for dom_handler and network_interceptor, where every call site in this file goes through a public method (query_elements, click_element, list_requests, get_cookies, etc.) with no reach-through to their internals observed.
fix_direction: Add the missing public methods (cleanup_all_tracked(), is_enabled(), get_browser_pids_for_profile()) to process_cleanup.py/debug_logger.py and switch these 3 call sites to use them.

what: _find_running_server() refuses to reuse a backend whose recorded version differs from _server_version(), which is importlib.metadata.version("stealth-chrome-devtools-mcp") — the installed distribution's frozen METADATA, not a hash or mtime of the source. pyproject.toml:7 pins a static `version = "1.2.0"` (no `dynamic = [...]`, no setuptools_scm/hatch-vcs). Verified against the live checkout: `pip show` reports this exact repo as an editable install at 1.2.0, `.venv/Lib/site-packages/stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp-1.2.0.dist-info/METADATA` says `Version: 1.2.0`, and `~/.stealth-mcp/server.json` right now records a live backend (pid 104504) at version "1.2.0" — while `git status` shows `M src/stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp/embedded/server.py` uncommitted in this same checkout. That edit is invisible to _server_version(); a client reconnect after the edit would still see version "1.2.0" match and proxy straight to the pre-edit process.
why_it_matters: This is the exact scenario the branch is named for and the exact friction already reported from real use: editing server.py and reconnecting does not get you fresh code, because the version check can only ever detect a bumped-and-reinstalled package version, never a source edit. tests/test_singleton_version_aware.py thoroughly proves the eviction LOGIC is correct (15+ tests), but every one of them monkeypatches _server_version() directly — none exercises whether the real function reflects a source change, because it structurally cannot. The only escapes are killing the backend process by hand or bump+reinstall; neither is automated or documented as required.
fix_direction: Either (cheap, S) document explicitly in singleton.py's module docstring and in CONTRIBUTING/dev docs that live server.py edits require killing the backend (`taskkill`/`kill` the pid in server.json) and that version bumps require reinstall to take effect; or (thorough, L) fold a content hash of the embedded/ package into the recorded state alongside the version string so dev-loop edits are auto-detected the same way version bumps are.
adv_rebuttal: The fact is airtight: _server_version() returns importlib.metadata.version (singleton.py:263-267), compared at L132, so a source edit at the frozen 1.2.0 is invisible on reconnect. But requiring a daemon restart to pick up source edits is normal persistent-process behavior, not a High defect; version-aware eviction correctly does its actual job (version bumps), and manual escapes exist. The branch name 'fix/singleton-version-aware-backend' shows it is already being worked. Severity inflated.

what: The `hot_reload` MCP tool (docstring: "Hot reload all modules without restarting the server") is the codebase's own purpose-built answer to F-120's gap, and it does not work. It never reloads server.py itself (where the actual tool implementations live) or cdp_function_executor.py — the fourth singleton declared in the identical server.py:1297-1300 block as browser_manager, silently excluded with no comment. For the modules it does reload, `browser_manager = BrowserManager()` (line 2997) constructs a fresh instance with an empty _instances dict, discarding every live browser session, _spawn_diagnostics entry, and _proxy_forwarder, and abandoning the running _idle_reaper_task uncancelled (it keeps running, closed over the now-orphaned old self). `grep -rn hot_reload tests/` returns zero matches — no test exercises this tool at all.
why_it_matters: A tool named exactly for "apply my edit without a restart", surfaced in the debugging section, will be reached for by anyone hitting F-120's friction. It silently fails to load the one file (server.py) that contains almost all real tool logic, silently skips a sibling singleton, and — if any browser instance is active when it's called — silently breaks that session (get_instance/navigate/close_instance all report "not found" afterward) while leaving the real Chrome process and idle-reaper task running headless, all while returning a success-looking string. Untested, so this has no regression protection at all.
fix_direction: Either remove hot_reload entirely and document that a full backend restart (kill + reconnect) is the only supported way to apply an edit, or fix it properly: reload every one of the 15 singleton-owning modules including server.py's own dependencies, migrate live _instances/_proxy_forwarders state into the new BrowserManager instance (or refuse to reload while instances are active), and cancel the old idle-reaper task before replacing browser_manager.
adv_rebuttal: Verified: the reload list (server.py:2983-2989) excludes both 'server' and 'cdp_function_executor' (the 4th singleton, declared alongside the others at 1300); line 2997 replaces browser_manager with an empty BrowserManager(), discarding _instances/_spawn_diagnostics; and `grep -rn hot_reload tests/` returns NO_MATCHES (untested). BROWSER_IDLE_TIMEOUT defaults to '0' (server.py:222), so the abandoned old idle reaper won't auto-close the orphaned browsers either.

what: The module is named persistent_storage.py and its singleton is imported everywhere as `persistent_storage`, but the class itself (honestly named InMemoryStorage) does zero disk I/O — it's a dict behind a lock, module-level singleton at persistent_storage.py:95. This is not an oversight the codebase is unaware of: server.py:1260-1276 (app_lifespan shutdown) explicitly calls `persistent_storage.clear_all()` on every graceful shutdown and logs "Clearing in-memory storage ... cleared", treating it as scoped to the current process on purpose. It's consulted only as a secondary cross-check in list_instances() (server.py:1422-1439), never as a source of truth to restore from.
why_it_matters: Everything tracked here — every browser instance's last-known state — is unconditionally lost on any restart, including every version-driven eviction this branch's own mechanism deliberately triggers (F-120/F-126). There is no cross-backend version isolation mechanism because there is no cross-backend persistence medium at all — trivially "isolated" only because nothing is shared. The concrete cost is misdirected effort: this audit's own brief had to ask "is it actually persistent" because the name says otherwise, and a future maintainer asked to add real durability could easily assume this module is most of the way there when it is architecturally the opposite.
fix_direction: Rename the module/variable to something honest (e.g. instance_registry.py / instance_registry) and drop "persistent" entirely, or — if real cross-restart durability is actually wanted for the version-eviction scenario — replace the backing dict with an on-disk store (sqlite/JSON file under STATE_DIR) and add a matching restore path that server.py's list_instances() checks alongside live memory.

what: DynamicHookSystem.__init__ (line 169) declares `self._lock = asyncio.Lock()`, and create_hook (line 353) / remove_hook (line 409) correctly mutate self.instance_hooks under `async with self._lock`. But add_instance/remove_instance (quoted here, called synchronously from browser_manager._setup_dynamic_hooks and _discard_instance_unlocked on every browser spawn/close) mutate the identical self.instance_hooks dict with no lock — they are plain `def`, so they structurally cannot take the asyncio lock even if intended to. More significantly, the hottest read path — setup_interception and _process_request_hooks, invoked once per intercepted network request/response as an independent asyncio.create_task per event (line 220-223) — read self.hooks/self.instance_hooks without ever acquiring the lock.
why_it_matters: The lock is applied at only 2 of the 6 touch points on this shared state, and the two it misses are the highest-frequency ones (per-request interception can fire many times per second per active browser instance). It currently avoids visible corruption only because CPython dict operations are atomic between await points, not because the design guarantees it — a future change that adds an await inside add_instance/remove_instance, or a burst of create_hook/remove_hook calls concurrent with heavy live traffic, has no synchronization protecting it. The lock's presence gives a false impression of thread/task-safety across the whole class.
fix_direction: Make add_instance/remove_instance async and take self._lock like their siblings; have setup_interception and _process_request_hooks snapshot self.hooks/self.instance_hooks under the lock before iterating, so all 6 touch points share one consistent discipline instead of 2 of 6.

quote:
# Read-only tooling (the `stealth-chrome-devtools` CLI) imports this
        # package to reuse profile helpers without taking over process
        # lifecycle. Honor an opt-out so importing never kills the running
        # server's browsers, deletes their profiles, or wipes PID tracking.
        if os.getenv("STEALTH_MCP_NO_AUTO_RECOVERY", "").strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}:
            return
        self._setup_cleanup_handlers()
        self._recover_orphaned_processes()
what: ProcessCleanup.__init__ runs `_recover_orphaned_processes()` by default, which loads `~/.stealth_browser_pids.json` (a real file in the developer's home directory, not test-isolated) and calls `_kill_processes_for_metadata(..., recovery=True)` — genuine psutil-based process termination and profile-directory cleanup — for any tracked entry that predates this process's `_init_time`. The module-level singleton `process_cleanup = ProcessCleanup()` (process_cleanup.py:1023) means this runs for real on the mere act of importing the module (directly, or transitively via `import browser_manager`). The comment quoted here is the code's own admission of the hazard. The only mitigation is `STEALTH_MCP_NO_AUTO_RECOVERY=1`: cli.py:35 sets it proactively for the CLI's `doctor` command, and exactly one test (test_singleton_fast_handshake.py:298) sets it — but only in a subprocess env dict, for a child process it spawns. tests/conftest.py, shared by all ~30 test files including test_process_cleanup.py which imports the class directly, never sets it for the pytest process itself.
why_it_matters: Any test run (or REPL session, or unrelated script) that imports browser_manager or process_cleanup in-process, on a developer machine that has ever left stale entries in ~/.stealth_browser_pids.json (e.g. after a crash or a force-killed terminal), triggers real process kills and profile deletions as a side effect of collection/import — not of any test actually calling the recovery code under test. It is silent and conditional (a no-op when the pid file doesn't exist, e.g. fresh CI checkouts), which is exactly the shape of hazard that never shows up in CI and only bites a specific developer's machine at an unpredictable time.
fix_direction: Add `os.environ.setdefault("STEALTH_MCP_NO_AUTO_RECOVERY", "1")` to tests/conftest.py before the sys.path insert (mirroring cli.py:35's pattern), and longer-term move `_recover_orphaned_processes()` out of __init__ into an explicit call the real entrypoint makes deliberately, so importing the module is never destructive regardless of caller.
adv_rebuttal: Real: ProcessCleanup.__init__ calls _recover_orphaned_processes() (process_cleanup.py:55) via the module singleton at L1023, and conftest.py does NOT set STEALTH_MCP_NO_AUTO_RECOVERY (verified absent). But it is triple-guarded - env-var opt-out at L52, _init_time so it never kills current-session processes (L45-47), and a no-op when the pid file is absent (so never on CI) - and it only reaps this tool's own tracked orphans. A genuine import-time side-effect smell, but High overstates a guarded, CI-invisible, self-scoped cleanup.

browser_manager = BrowserManager()
network_interceptor = NetworkInterceptor()
dom_handler = DOMHandler()
cdp_function_executor = CDPFunctionExecutor()
what: All 15 embedded/ singletons (this block plus persistent_storage, dynamic_hook_system, dynamic_hook_ai, element_cloner, process_cleanup, progressive_element_cloner, comprehensive_element_cloner, response_handler, debug_logger, hook_learning_system, file_based_element_cloner) construct unconditionally at first import, for the lifetime of the interpreter, with no factory function, dependency-injection seam, or reset hook anywhere. Combined with Python's `from x import y` name-binding semantics (each importer gets its own bound reference at import time, not a live lookup), no code path — including hot_reload, see F-121 — can cleanly swap all holders of a reference to a fresh instance.
why_it_matters: tests/conftest.py works around this rather than through it: it pre-sets STEALTH_MCP_CLONE_OUTPUT_DIR before any import can happen specifically because the module-global ResponseHandler()/FileBasedElementCloner() create their output dir at import time (conftest.py's own comment), and individual test files construct their own fresh class instances (e.g. test_process_cleanup.py's `ProcessCleanup()` per test) instead of exercising the shared singleton — which sidesteps the problem for that one class but leaves the real module-level singleton's state (constructed for real the moment any test file first imports it) never reset between tests and never verified clean. The architecture provides none of the isolation the singleton pattern is usually chosen for, and the workaround is inconsistently applied across the 15.
fix_direction: Convert the 15 module-level singletons to explicit factory functions (get_browser_manager(), get_persistent_storage(), ...) with a process-level cache that tests can clear via a single reset_all_singletons() helper called from a conftest.py autouse fixture; this also gives hot_reload a real mechanism to invalidate and rebuild state instead of the current ad hoc global-rebinding dance.

what: _clear_stale_backend's eviction of a stale-version backend calls psutil.Process.terminate()/kill() directly from the new session's proxy process. On the platform this repo is actually developed on (Windows — confirmed via env), psutil.Process.terminate() is documented to map to the Win32 TerminateProcess() API, which does not run the target's Python signal handlers or atexit hooks — unlike POSIX SIGTERM, which process_cleanup.py:187-188 explicitly registers a handler for (`signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, self._signal_handler)`). This means the stale backend's own FastMCP app_lifespan graceful-shutdown chain (server.py:1250-1277: browser_manager.close_all(), process_cleanup._cleanup_all_tracked(), persistent_storage.clear_all()) likely never runs on eviction on Windows.
why_it_matters: If accurate, every version-driven eviction on Windows abruptly kills the old backend process without giving it a chance to close its own tracked Chrome instances cleanly, leaving that cleanup entirely to the next backend's _recover_orphaned_processes() startup pass — which is exactly the import-time-triggered, opt-out-gated mechanism flagged as fragile in F-124. The new backend's clean start is only as reliable as that recovery pass. This finding is based on documented OS/psutil termination semantics rather than a directly observed eviction-in-progress trace, so confidence is lower than the others in this set.
fix_direction: Before the hard terminate()/kill(), attempt a graceful shutdown first (e.g. POST a shutdown/close request to the stale backend's own HTTP endpoint, or send a CTRL_BREAK_EVENT on Windows via CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP, which the process already sets at spawn time in _start_server_process) and fall back to terminate()/kill() only after a short grace period times out.

quote:
Enhanced Element Cloner using proper CDP methods
=================================================

This module provides comprehensive element extraction using the full power of
Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) through nodriver. It extracts:

1. Complete computed styles using CDP CSS.getComputedStyleForNode
2. Matched CSS rules using CDP CSS.getMatchedStylesForNode  
3. Event listeners using CDP DOMDebugger.getEventListeners
4. All stylesheet information via CDP CSS domain
5. Complete DOM structure and attributes

This provides 100% accurate element cloning by using CDP's native capabilities
instead of limited JavaScript-based extraction.
"""
what: Three independently-implemented engines all answer 'extract complete data about this element': ElementCloner.clone_element_complete (asyncio.gather over 6 JS-file evaluations, element_cloner.py:464-539), ComprehensiveElementCloner.extract_complete_element (one 216-line monolithic inline JS string, comprehensive_element_cloner.py:62-278), and CDPElementCloner.extract_complete_element_cdp (raw CDP domain calls, cdp_element_cloner.py:33-93). All three are live, each wired to its own MCP tool, and each returns a structurally different result schema (flat multi-key vs. flat camelCase vs. nested snake_case under an 'element' key). CDPElementCloner's own module docstring explicitly claims it supersedes the JS-based approaches ('100% accurate ... instead of limited JavaScript-based extraction'), yet the other two were never removed or rerouted to call it.
why_it_matters: Any bug fix, new field, or behavior change to 'how we extract element data' must be manually triplicated across 3 unrelated code paths using 3 different browser-automation techniques (JS-file injection, monolithic inline JS, raw CDP). This has already happened partially (see F-142): a hang-avoidance rewrite reached only 1 of the 5 sibling primitives. Consumers also get inconsistent response shapes depending on which of the near-synonymous tool names they picked, with no indication which is canonical.
fix_direction: Pick one engine (CDP-native, per the code's own stated rationale) as canonical, reimplement the JS-file and monolithic-JS variants as thin adapters over it or delete them outright, and converge the 3 output schemas into 1. Keep the 16 tool names as thin call-throughs if backward compatibility for tool discovery matters; the response schema changes regardless.
adv_rebuttal: Verified three live, tool-wired 'extract complete element' engines with different schemas: ElementCloner.clone_element_complete, ComprehensiveElementCloner.extract_complete_element, and CDPElementCloner.extract_complete_element_cdp - whose own docstring claims it supersedes the others, yet the other two remain wired to tools. Real triplication. Overlaps F-203/F-601 (5-module framing) but is a distinct sub-claim about the 3 complete-extract engines.

what: extract_element_styles_cdp's docstring states its CDP-native rewrite exists specifically to prevent hangs, and extract_element_styles (element_cloner.py:28-65) now does nothing but delegate to it. But the other 4 extraction primitives in the same ElementCloner class - extract_element_structure, extract_element_events, extract_element_animations, extract_element_assets - still call self._load_js_file(...) followed by await tab.evaluate(js_code) (e.g. element_cloner.py:152-153), the exact JS-evaluation pattern the CDP rewrite was built to replace.
why_it_matters: This is a fix that landed in 1 of 5 sibling methods and was never propagated to the other 4. If the underlying hang bug is real (the docstring states it as fact, not hypothesis), 4 of 5 extraction tools in ElementCloner - extract_element_structure, extract_element_events, extract_element_animations, extract_element_assets - remain exposed to it, with no tracking issue or TODO marking the gap.
fix_direction: Port extract_element_structure/events/animations/assets to CDP-native calls following the extract_element_styles_cdp pattern (DOM domain for structure/children, DOMDebugger.getEventListeners for events), removing the _load_js_file/tab.evaluate path entirely once parity is confirmed.

The '1 of 5 fixed, 4 exposed' claim is true; the only imprecision is that assets inlines JS rather than calling _load_js_file, but the hang exposure is identical.

what: clone_element_progressive only ever calls comprehensive_element_cloner.extract_complete_element (progressive_element_cloner.py:40-42), whose output is flat (top-level 'styles', 'eventListeners', 'cssRules'). Yet this block, and the same .get('element',{}).get(X) or .get(Y) fallback pattern repeated in expand_styles (93-104), expand_events (141-146), and list_stored_elements (231-248), defends against the nested CDP-shaped schema ('element.computed_styles', 'element.event_listeners') that only CDPElementCloner produces - a producer that is never wired into ProgressiveElementCloner.
why_it_matters: Dead defensive branches for a data source that is never actually connected are a fossil of an abandoned or planned engine swap. Beyond the maintenance confusion, the 'or'/ternary fallback silently returns 0 or {} on a genuine shape mismatch instead of surfacing an error, which would hide a real regression (e.g. if extract_complete_element's schema changes) behind a plausible-looking empty result.
fix_direction: Once F-140's schema consolidation lands, delete the dual-schema .get(...) or .get(...) fallbacks throughout progressive_element_cloner.py and read the single canonical shape directly; let a genuine shape mismatch raise instead of silently returning empty.

what: element_cloner, comprehensive_element_cloner, file_based_element_cloner, and progressive_element_cloner are all module-level singletons constructed once at import time (server.py imports the pre-built instances directly). CDPElementCloner is the only one of the 5 imported as a class and instantiated fresh inside the tool function body on every single call. Separately, FileBasedElementCloner.__init__ (file_based_element_cloner.py:48) constructs its own private ComprehensiveElementCloner() instance instead of importing the shared comprehensive_element_cloner singleton that comprehensive_element_cloner.py already builds at module scope.
why_it_matters: Currently harmless only because both classes' __init__ methods are no-ops (CDPElementCloner.__init__ is 'pass'; ComprehensiveElementCloner.__init__ is 'pass'). But it is an inconsistent construction pattern across otherwise-parallel classes: the next person to add caching, connection reuse, or any per-instance state to CDPElementCloner or ComprehensiveElementCloner will silently get per-call or duplicate-instance semantics instead of the singleton behavior every sibling class relies on.
fix_direction: Instantiate cdp_cloner = CDPElementCloner() once at module scope alongside the other 4 singletons and import it in server.py; have FileBasedElementCloner accept/import the shared comprehensive_element_cloner singleton instead of constructing its own.
