what: No test in the repository exercises the extraction logic of any of the 5 cloner classes (ElementCloner.extract_*, ComprehensiveElementCloner.extract_complete_element, ProgressiveElementCloner.clone_element_progressive/expand_*, CDPElementCloner.extract_complete_element_cdp, or any FileBasedElementCloner method's actual extraction path). Only 3 files reference these classes/instances by name (test_element_cloner_output_dir.py, test_clone_output_dir.py, conftest.py) and all 3 test only FileBasedElementCloner's output-directory path resolution. The other 5 clone-named test files (test_clone_storage_cap.py, test_clone_sweep_race.py, test_clone_trash_recovery.py, test_clone_legacy_marker_classification.py, test_profile_clone_excludes_cache.py) import disk-hygiene helpers directly from server (_clone_is_auto, _enforce_clone_storage_cap_in, _profile_ignore_names, _copy_profile_delta) - a storage-eviction subsystem layered on top of the clone output directory, unrelated to whether extraction itself is correct.
why_it_matters: The consolidation this deep-dive recommends (F-140/F-141/F-142) would be executed with zero automated signal on whether the unified extractor still returns equivalent data to the 3 engines it replaces. Given the priority order for this audit is maintainability first, the highest-leverage fix here (collapsing 3 extraction engines into 1) is also the riskiest to do blind.
fix_direction: Before or alongside consolidation, add extraction-correctness tests against a mocked tab/CDP surface (mirroring the mocking style test_server_call_conventions.py already establishes) covering at minimum: element found/not-found, styles/events/children shape, and the schema each of the 3 engines currently produces, so a consolidation PR can diff old-vs-new output.
adv_rebuttal: Accurate: the only cloner tests cover FileBasedElementCloner output-dir resolution and server-level storage-hygiene helpers; nothing exercises any extraction path of the 5 cloner classes. That makes the F-140/F-203 consolidation genuinely blind. Cannot refute.

what: AI-authored hook code is exec'd (line 84) with a hand-rolled restricted __builtins__ dict, and requirements['custom_condition'] is eval'd (line 123) with an even smaller one ({'len','str'}). Both are the sole containment for two separate exec/eval surfaces; the eval site additionally receives the live RequestInfo (real URL/headers/method of an actual network event) as a local variable at match time, once per intercepted request. The only static gate, hook_learning_system.validate_hook_function (ast.parse/ast.walk, hook_learning_system.py:519-543), denylists bare-name calls to eval/exec/open/input via `isinstance(node.func, ast.Name)` — and per dynamic_hook_ai_interface.py:39 it is only ever run against function_code, never against custom_condition, so the eval() site has zero static gating at all.
why_it_matters: Restricted __builtins__ dicts do not sandbox Python: attribute-chain gadgets like `().__class__.__bases__[0].__subclasses__()` reach arbitrary loaded classes (and, through their __init__.__globals__, arbitrary modules) without ever calling a bare builtin name, so they evade both the restricted dict and the AST denylist. The code text here is always AI/operator-authored (never page-controlled — see deepdive_codeexec.md), so this is a self-inflicted surface, not remote code execution. But the module presents itself as sandboxed (restricted dict + a validator function literally named validate_hook_function), which is a false assurance that could mislead a future maintainer into trusting this boundary for something more sensitive than it can actually hold, and the custom_condition path doesn't even get the weak check the docs imply exists.
fix_direction: Either (a) stop pretending this is a sandbox — document plainly that hook code runs with full local privileges once past syntax validation, and drop the false-confidence framing; or (b) actually contain it: run compiled hook functions in a separate low-privilege subprocess, or replace free-form Python with a constrained declarative rule format (the requirements dict already covers the common cases). At minimum, route custom_condition through validate_hook_function too, since right now it gets strictly less scrutiny than function_code despite running on every matched network event.

what: The create_python_binding MCP tool execs the caller-supplied python_code string with `exec_globals = {}`. Since that dict has no '__builtins__' key, CPython auto-populates it with a reference to the real builtins module before execution (documented exec() behavior) — meaning this code runs with completely unrestricted builtins: open, __import__, eval, exec, everything. There is no restricted-namespace attempt, no AST validation, nothing — in contrast to the sibling exec/eval sites in dynamic_hook_system.py (F-160), which at least attempt a restricted __builtins__ dict and route through an AST denylist.
why_it_matters: Same trust boundary as F-160 (python_code is always an MCP tool-call argument, i.e. AI/operator-authored, never page-controlled), but the blast radius is materially larger: this runs in the MCP server's own long-lived Python process with the operator's full OS-user privileges (filesystem, environment variables, ability to spawn subprocesses), not inside a purpose-built hook namespace. It's also a silent capability gap — nothing about the tool's docstring ('Python function code (as string)') signals that it carries zero sandboxing, while a maintainer skimming the codebase could reasonably assume the restricted-builtins pattern used elsewhere for hook code is the house style. That inconsistency is itself a maintainability smell: two modules solving the same problem (let AI-written Python run) with wildly different (and both broken) postures.
fix_direction: Reuse the same constrained-namespace pattern already implemented in dynamic_hook_system.DynamicHook._compile_function (or better, a shared helper) instead of a bare `{}` globals dict, and run python_code through hook_learning_system.validate_hook_function (or an equivalent check) before exec. This won't make it a real sandbox (see F-160's caveat) but it closes the gap between 'no attempt at all' and 'the same weak attempt used elsewhere,' which is the more urgent inconsistency to fix first.
adv_rebuttal: Same code as F-107 (exec_globals={}; exec at server.py:3847-3848) and the same operator-authored trust boundary - the finding itself states python_code is 'never page-controlled,' so RCE severity is self-inflicted in this local tool. Its factual sub-claim checks out: dynamic_hook_system.py does use a restricted __builtins__ (L77, L121) and imports ast, so the cross-module inconsistency smell is real but minor. Downgrade the security framing; keep as a low maintainability note.

what: _translate_python_to_js (cdp_function_executor.py:707-740) tries py2js.convert() first (a real declared dependency, pyproject.toml:52) but catches bare `except Exception` around it (line 738), not just ImportError — so any input py2js can't handle silently falls through to _fallback_python_to_js, a line-by-line blind substring replacer. That fallback replaces the substrings True/False/None/print(/.append( anywhere they appear, including inside string literals or as substrings of identifiers (e.g. a variable is_True_flag, or the string literal "True Detective"), and has no handling for Python block syntax (for/while/if bodies, indentation) at all. The caller gets no indication that the lossy fallback path — rather than the real py2js translation — was used for a given call.
why_it_matters: execute_python_in_browser markets itself as running 'Python code'; in the primary path it's really 'Python transpiled by a third-party library into JS run in the page,' which is a reasonable design. But the silent fallback means any py2js limitation (not just a missing install) degrades to a translator that can produce syntactically valid but semantically wrong JavaScript — worst case, quietly wrong behavior rather than a clear error, for any Python snippet containing string literals or identifiers that overlap the replacement substrings.
fix_direction: Narrow the except clause around py2js.convert() to only catch the specific translation-failure exception(s) py2js raises, not bare Exception; when the fallback path is used, surface that in the tool's returned result (e.g. `"translator": "fallback"`) so callers can distinguish a best-effort lossy translation from the real one; at minimum make the substring replacements word-boundary-aware (regex \b) instead of blind .replace().

what: _process_request_hooks's own docstring (line 270, read verbatim: "Process hooks for a request/response in real-time with priority chain processing.") and the AI-facing best-practices doc (hook_learning_system.py:465: "Use priority (lower = higher priority) to control hook execution order") both describe multiple hooks composing in priority order. The implementation sorts all matching_hooks by priority (line 280) and logs how many matched (quoted above), but then takes only `matching_hooks[0]` and calls _execute_hook_action exactly once. Every hook ranked below the top match for a given request never executes at all — not throttled, not merged, just silently skipped every single time, for the lifetime of the hook.
why_it_matters: An operator or the AI itself, reading the docs, would reasonably register two hooks on overlapping URL patterns expecting both to contribute (e.g. one adds security headers, another logs) — priority is documented as ordering, not exclusivity. What actually happens is silent: no error, no warning in list_hooks/get_hook_details, the lower-priority hook just never fires. This is a debugging trap — trigger_count for the shadowed hook stays at 0 forever with no indication why.
fix_direction: Cheapest correct fix: align the docs/docstring to actual behavior (first-match-wins by priority, not a chain) and surface shadowing in list_hooks/get_hook_details (e.g. an `is_shadowed_by` field when a higher-priority hook with an overlapping pattern exists on the same instance). Implementing true multi-hook chaining is a larger design decision (action-merge semantics across hooks) and should be scoped separately if actually wanted.

quote:
async def _with_cdp_timeout(coro, timeout: float = 0, instance_id: str = ""):
    """Wrap a CDP coroutine with asyncio.wait_for to prevent infinite hangs.

    When a Chrome DevTools Protocol connection is stale or dead, awaiting a
    CDP operation blocks forever.  This wrapper raises a clear error after
    *timeout* seconds so the caller (and the MCP client) gets a response
    instead of hanging indefinitely.
    """
    t = timeout or CDP_OPERATION_TIMEOUT
    try:
        return await asyncio.wait_for(coro, timeout=t)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        tag = f" (instance {instance_id})" if instance_id else ""
        raise Exception(
            f"CDP operation timed out after {t:.0f}s{tag}. "
what: _with_cdp_timeout (used by nearly every CDP-calling tool, including call_javascript_function, inject_and_execute_script, create_persistent_function) and cdp_function_executor.execute_python_in_browser's own local `asyncio.wait_for(..., timeout=10.0)` both give up waiting on the Python side when a CDP call takes too long, but neither issues Runtime.terminateExecution (or any other command) to actually stop the browser-side script. cdp_function_executor.list_cdp_commands() (line 117) even enumerates terminateExecution as a known Runtime command — it's cataloged for the AI to call manually, never invoked automatically on this path.
why_it_matters: Cancelling the Python-side await does not stop a synchronous, blocking script (e.g. an infinite loop) running on the tab's JS main thread — that's a browser/CDP fact independent of this codebase. The docstring's framing ('prevent infinite hangs,' 'timeout protection') implies the operation is recovered on timeout; in reality the caller gets an error back but the tab itself stays wedged. Every subsequent CDP call targeting that same tab (screenshots, clicks, further evaluates, hook interception setup) will also hang and eventually time out the same way, until the whole browser instance is closed and respawned — a real operability trap for a tool whose core use case is iterative, interactive browser automation. Scoped to the one wedged tab, not the whole server, since other instances/tabs proceed independently.
fix_direction: On asyncio.TimeoutError in both wrappers, before raising/returning the error, issue `tab.send(uc.cdp.runtime.terminate_execution())` (already enumerated in list_cdp_commands) to actually attempt recovery of the tab; if that itself fails/hangs, surface a clearer 'tab is likely wedged, consider closing and respawning this instance' message instead of the current generic timeout text.
adv_rebuttal: Mechanism confirmed: _with_cdp_timeout (server.py:139-156) and execute_python_in_browser's wait_for(10.0) (cdp_function_executor.py:696-698) only raise on timeout; terminateExecution appears solely in the list_cdp_commands catalog (114), never invoked. But High is over-severe: the wrapper bounds the wait and its error message explicitly tells the caller to close_instance + respawn (155), the finding itself concedes the un-cancellable JS loop is an intrinsic CDP fact, and Runtime.terminateExecution is not a reliable interrupt for a synchronous loop anyway. Scoped to one recoverable tab.

what: The same dict[str,str] -> List[HeaderEntry] conversion loop shown here is repeated near-verbatim two more times in the same method: the modify/response branch (lines 474-477, variable name response_headers) and the modify/request branch (lines 486-489, variable name headers again). _execute_hook_action (435-519, 85 lines) and _process_request_hooks (269-344, 76 lines) each mix CDP-protocol plumbing, action-dispatch business logic, and error-recovery fallback in single long methods with deep branching, consistent with the D-grade cyclomatic-complexity flag called out for review.
why_it_matters: Three copies of the same 4-line conversion is a small but concrete driver of the complexity score, and a latent bug risk: a future change to header-entry construction (e.g. handling duplicate header names, or case normalization) has to be remembered and applied in three places, and it's easy to update two and miss the third.
fix_direction: Extract a `_headers_dict_to_entries(headers: Optional[Dict[str,str]]) -> List[HeaderEntry]` helper and call it from all three branches; consider also splitting _execute_hook_action's if/elif chain into one small function per action (block/fulfill/redirect/modify) to bring both methods' complexity down independently of the header-loop fix.

what: create_simple_dynamic_hook's built-in 'log' template generates a process_request body that does `import sys` before using it. DynamicHook._compile_function's restricted __builtins__ dict (dynamic_hook_system.py:73-82) does not include '__import__', which the `import` statement requires at the point it executes. Since `import sys` sits inside the function body (not at exec-time module level), it only runs when the hook actually fires on a matching request — at which point it raises, and DynamicHook.process()'s broad `except Exception` (dynamic_hook_system.py:158-160) swallows it into HookAction(action="continue"), logging only a debug_logger.log_error line.
why_it_matters: This is one of exactly four ready-made convenience actions the 'easier for AI' simple-hook tool offers (block/redirect/add_headers/log), and it is silently non-functional every single time it's used: the hook always falls through to 'continue' and never logs anything, with no error surfaced to the caller — only a debug log entry that's easy to miss in normal operation. A one-line unit test invoking this template through DynamicHook.process() would have caught it immediately.
fix_direction: Drop the `import sys` / `file=sys.stderr` and just use the `print(...)` already provided in the restricted namespace (dynamic_hook_system.py:80, which routes to debug_logger.log_info) — a one-line template fix. Add a regression test that runs each create_simple_dynamic_hook template through DynamicHook.process() with a sample request and asserts no swallowed exception.

what: close_instance() wraps its whole teardown (_do_close, browser_manager.py:617-757) in asyncio.wait_for(timeout=5.0), which reads as a hard bound. It is not one, because the calls that actually do the work are synchronous and never yield to the event loop, so asyncio's cooperative cancellation can never preempt them. Inside _do_close: process_cleanup.kill_browser_process(instance_id) (line 682) and process_cleanup.finalize_browser_process/cleanup_deferred_profiles (lines 743-744) are called directly, not via asyncio.to_thread/run_in_executor (confirmed by grep: the only to_thread usage anywhere in embedded/ is in server.py for unrelated storage sweeps and snapshot refresh -- browser_manager.py and process_cleanup.py use it nowhere). kill_browser_process -> _kill_processes_for_metadata loops sequentially over every PID sharing the instance's --user-data-dir (routinely more than one: Chrome's GPU/renderer/utility children inherit the same --user-data-dir flag), and _kill_process_by_pid does psutil terminate()+wait(timeout=3) then kill()+wait(timeout=2) per PID -- individually bounded, but purely synchronous, so N unresponsive processes block the single-threaded event loop for up to 5*N seconds with no way for the 5.0s wait_for timer to fire in between (the timer callback cannot run while the loop is stuck inside a synchronous call). browser.stop() (_stop_browser, line 688) is similarly unguarded, in contrast to the CDP send/disconnect calls just above it (lines 654-657, 668-670) which the same function DOES wrap in their own asyncio.wait_for(timeout=2.0) -- showing the authors knew to bound awaitable CDP ops but missed that the synchronous process-kill/profile-cleanup path needed the same treatment. The quoted fallback (triggered when wait_for actually does fire, e.g. if the hang occurred at an awaitable point) makes it worse: it re-invokes the exact same unbounded synchronous calls (kill_browser_process, finalize_browser_process, cleanup_deferred_profiles) a second time, now with no timeout wrapper at all.
why_it_matters: close_instance is not a shutdown-only helper -- it is registered as a live MCP tool (server.py:1453, calling browser_manager.close_instance at line 1469) that any connected client can invoke at any time. Because this codebase now runs as a singleton backend shared by every concurrent Claude Code session (singleton.py), one client calling close_instance against an unresponsive/wedged Chrome process freezes the entire event loop -- and therefore every other session's tool calls, every other browser instance, and the idle reaper -- for as long as the synchronous kill sequence takes, which is unbounded in the worst case (multiple hung child processes, or a nodriver browser.stop() that blocks on a dead subprocess handle). This is precisely the 3am scenario: an operator sees the whole server stop responding, not just one instance failing to close, and the advertised 'timeout=5.0' gives false confidence that this cannot happen.
fix_direction: Move the synchronous process_cleanup calls (kill_browser_process, finalize_browser_process, cleanup_deferred_profiles) and _stop_browser's browser.stop() off the event loop via asyncio.to_thread, so asyncio.wait_for's cancellation can actually preempt them; keep the outer 5.0s (or similar) budget around the to_thread call itself so a genuinely stuck kill sequence still returns control to the loop. Apply the same to the TimeoutError fallback path so a second hang can't recur unbounded.
adv_rebuttal: The core claim survives exactly as the scrutiny predicted: kill_browser_process (browser_manager.py:682), _stop_browser->browser.stop() (688->134-136) and finalize/cleanup (743-744) are synchronous under wait_for(5.0) at 760 — NOT offloaded to a thread — so the 5s timer cannot preempt them, and the fallback (768-770) re-runs them unwrapped. But 'unbounded total server hang' is contradicted by the code: _kill_process_by_pid's waits are explicitly bounded (process_cleanup.py:880 wait(timeout=3), 902 wait(timeout=2)), and on Windows terminate()==kill()==TerminateProcess is immediate, so the typical freeze is sub-second and the worst case is bounded ~5s x N-pids, not infinite. Real event-loop-blocking bug, over-severe framing.

what: embedded/ contains 176 `except Exception` handlers across 20 of 23 .py files (server.py 22, browser_manager.py 21, cdp_function_executor.py 15, dom_handler.py 15, dynamic_hook_system.py 15, network_interceptor.py 14, process_cleanup.py 14, and 13 more files with 1-11 each) and zero bare `except:`. 143 of the 176 do call debug_logger.log_error/log_warning or re-raise. The remaining 22 swallow the exception completely: no logging call, no re-raise, and the exception object itself is never referenced anywhere in the handler body -- e.g. switch_to_tab (quoted) and close_tab (browser_manager.py:1204-1208) both collapse any failure to a bare `return False`; network_interceptor.py:171/357 discard response-body read errors with only an inline comment; proxy_utils.py:109/124 mask redaction failures; singleton.py has four such handlers (lines 268, 293, 358, 361) discussed separately in F-183. This is one pattern (defensive except-Exception with an inconsistent logging discipline), not 22 independent bugs.
why_it_matters: For the truly silent 22, a caller (often the MCP tool wrapper) sees only `False`/`{}`/`continue`, with no way to distinguish 'tab already gone' from 'CDP call timed out' from 'browser process died mid-call'. In browser_manager.py specifically, switch_to_tab and close_tab sit directly in the tab-lifecycle hot path this deep-dive covers -- a flaky tab switch during an automation run degrades to an unexplained `False` with nothing in the debug view to correlate against. The other 154 instances that do call debug_logger are only as useful as debug_logger itself, which F-182 shows is disabled by default -- so in a stock deployment the effective silent-swallow rate is close to 176/176, not 22/176.
fix_direction: For the 22 truly-silent handlers, add at minimum a debug_logger.log_warning with the exception repr before returning the failure sentinel -- these are cheap, mechanical edits. Consider a lint rule (or a small wrapper/decorator) that flags any `except Exception` block whose body never references the bound exception name, so new silent swallows can't creep back in.

quote:
def log_error(self, component: str, method: str, error: Exception, context: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None):
        """
        Log an error with full context.

        Args:
            component (str): Name of the component where the error occurred.
            method (str): Name of the method where the error occurred.
            error (Exception): The exception instance.
            context (Optional[Dict[str, Any]]): Additional context for the error.
        """
        if not self._enabled:
            return
what: DebugLogger.__init__ sets self._enabled = False (debug_logger.py:41), and log_error/log_warning/log_info (lines 72-73, 111-112, 138-139) each open with `if not self._enabled: return` -- before anything is appended to the in-memory buffers, not just before the stderr print. self._enabled only flips True via server.py:1303 (`if DEBUG_LOGGING_ENABLED: debug_logger.enable()`), where DEBUG_LOGGING_ENABLED (server.py:217-220) is itself `parse_bool_env("STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG", False) or parse_bool_env("DEBUG", False)` -- opt-in via environment variable (or the equivalent --debug CLI flag at server.py:4123-4128), off by default. Grepping the whole embedded/ package for `force=True` (the one parameter that bypasses the gate, on _emit_stderr) turns up exactly one call site: the logger announcing to itself that it was just enabled (debug_logger.py:303). No error/warning/info call anywhere uses it. Separately, even when enabled, storage is purely in-process Python lists capped at MAX_ERRORS=500/MAX_WARNINGS=1000/MAX_INFO=2000 (a trimming ring buffer, debug_logger.py:18-20, 96-97) -- there is no log file written automatically; persistence only happens if something explicitly calls export_to_file/export_to_file_paginated.
why_it_matters: Every one of the 176 except-Exception handlers audited in F-181, all of close_instance's teardown diagnostics (F-180), and process_cleanup's kill/recovery logging all funnel through this gate. In the out-of-the-box configuration (no STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG/DEBUG env var, no --debug flag -- the default a user or an MCP host config would run with), none of it produces any output: not to stderr, not to the in-memory buffers that export_debug_logs/get_debug_view read from. A 3am hang or crash investigation on a default install finds the debug view completely empty, regardless of how carefully instrumented the surrounding code is -- and since nothing is ever flushed to disk on its own, a hard crash or force-kill loses whatever *would* have been buffered even if logging had been on.
fix_direction: Default STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG-equivalent logging to on for warnings/errors (keep verbose info-level logging opt-in if volume is a concern), or at minimum have log_error always record to the in-memory ring buffer regardless of _enabled (only gate the stderr echo on the flag) so get_debug_view/export_debug_logs have something to show after the fact. Consider an automatic periodic or on-shutdown flush of the error buffer to a small rotating file under the existing ~/.stealth-mcp state dir so a crash doesn't discard it.
adv_rebuttal: Facts are exact: _enabled=False by default (debug_logger.py:41); log_error/warning/info return at 72/111/138 BEFORE the buffer append; force=True occurs only at 303 (enable's self-announcement); no automatic file write. But 'off by default' is a standard, defensible logging design, the toggle is documented (README:212 / --debug), and once enabled the in-memory buffers ARE retrievable via get_debug_view — so this is a Medium observability default for a single-user tool, not a High defect.

what: singleton.py never imports debug_logger; its only diagnostic output in 571 lines is two raw print(..., file=sys.stderr) calls (lines 516-520, 530-534), both inside monitor_backend/run_backend_guarded for a backend that WAS healthy and later died. The cold-start path has no equivalent. ensure_server_running (line 297) fires _start_backend_holding_lock in a daemon thread and returns immediately without waiting on it (by design, so the stdio handshake never blocks). If anything in that thread throws -- _start_server_process failing to launch (bad interpreter path, a syntax error in a freshly-edited server.py causing the subprocess to exit immediately), _clear_stale_backend failing to evict a stale port-holder, or any other exception -- the quoted except swallows it completely: no log, no print, no re-raise, and since it's a daemon thread wrapped in its own try/except, Python's default unhandled-thread-exception traceback (which would otherwise print to stderr) never fires either. Meanwhile run_backend() (singleton.py:459-461) does `if not await _await_backend_http(url): return` -- and _await_backend_http polls for the full BACKEND_READY_TIMEOUT (120.0s, line 47), with each individual probe's own exception also silently caught (lines 358, 361). When it finally gives up, run_backend returns normally (no exception), so run_backend_guarded's `except Exception` (which is the only branch that prints anything, line 515-520) never triggers -- only its unconditional `finally: tg.cancel_scope.cancel()` (line 521-522) runs, tearing the proxy down with zero explanation.
why_it_matters: This is the exact failure mode the fix/singleton-version-aware-backend branch's own feature depends on: when server.py is edited, the singleton is supposed to detect the version mismatch and start a fresh backend. If that fresh start fails for any reason, the operator sees the client hang for up to 120 seconds and then silently disconnect -- with absolutely nothing in stderr, no debug_logger entry (there is no integration to have one), and no way to tell 'the new backend crashed on startup' apart from 'the old backend is slow to answer' apart from any other cause. This is a foundational blind spot in precisely the workflow (edit server.py, expect a fresh backend process) already known to be a pain point.
fix_direction: Import debug_logger (or at minimum print to stderr, matching the existing pattern in monitor_backend/run_backend_guarded) inside _start_backend_holding_lock's except block, including the captured exception. Have run_backend print/log explicitly when _await_backend_http exhausts its deadline instead of returning silently, so the teardown in run_backend_guarded has something to report even without an exception in flight.
adv_rebuttal: Confirmed silent cold-start: ensure_server_running returns immediately (singleton.py:297-313); _start_backend_holding_lock swallows everything via `except Exception: pass` (293-294) in a daemon thread; and on the proxy side run_backend returns silently after _await_backend_http's 120s timeout (460-461), so run_backend_guarded's only print (515-520) never fires — just the unconditional finally cancel (521-522). One nuance: a server.py syntax error won't raise inside Popen; it dies as a subprocess and is hidden by the ignored _wait_for_server return (292) instead — but the net 'silent failure' claim holds via multiple paths.

what: ResponseHandler.handle_response (response_handler.py:65-119) writes a brand-new, uniquely-named JSON file into self.clone_dir (default Path.home()/'.stealth-mcp'/'element_clones', response_handler.py:25) every time a response exceeds max_tokens (20000 by default). Nothing in handle_response, __init__, or anywhere else in this file checks existing directory size, file count, or age before writing, and nothing ever deletes these files. There are 8 call sites in server.py (screenshot capture ~line 2038/2963, network request dumps ~line 2087, element asset extraction ~line 2845, related-file extraction ~line 2917, expand_children ~line 3154, plus two more ~lines 3650/3701), so this is a routine, not exceptional, code path. Grepping the whole embedded/ package, 'element_clones' appears exactly once -- the path definition itself; no other file references it. This directory is distinct from the storage this codebase DOES actively manage: _run_storage_sweep/_enforce_clone_storage_cap_in (server.py:554-782) target _clone_root_dir() = <session-root>/'sessions' (a different path entirely), and structurally only recognize clone directories carrying a .stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp_clone.json marker (_clone_is_named, server.py:594-606) -- flat *.json response-spillover files would not be recognized as sweep targets even if they happened to share a root. The manual cleanup_clone_files MCP tool (server.py:3533-3546) is a third, separate path again: it delegates entirely to file_based_element_cloner.cleanup_old_files, unrelated to response_handler.clone_dir.
why_it_matters: Under the pre-singleton model (one backend process per session) this would self-limit at process lifetime. Under the new singleton architecture (singleton.py) one backend process now persists indefinitely across every session on the machine, so this directory accumulates for the life of the machine, not the life of a session -- every oversized screenshot, network capture, or element-asset dump is a permanent, unmanaged file. There is no automatic mechanism, and no manual tool, that reclaims this specific directory; an operator noticing disk pressure would need to know to look at (and manually clear) ~/.stealth-mcp/element_clones specifically, since neither of the two cleanup mechanisms that do exist in this codebase touches it.
fix_direction: Either fold element_clones into the existing _run_storage_sweep pass (age/count-based eviction of the flat *.json files, mirroring the cap logic already built for the sessions/ clone root) or give ResponseHandler its own lightweight cap: on write, opportunistically delete the oldest files in clone_dir once total size/count exceeds a threshold. Either way, wire it into the periodic/background sweep the codebase already runs rather than leaving it to manual disk inspection.

what: The ONLY liveness signal in the whole system is a raw TCP connect/close. It is used in three places: _find_running_server() (which status/doctor and every session's ensure_server_running() call to decide whether to reuse the existing backend), _watch_backend_liveness() (the sole automatic-recovery watchdog, which tears the proxy down after 3 consecutive failures so the client reconnects to a fresh backend), and _clear_stale_backend()'s early-return guard (the only eviction path). None of the three ever verifies the backend's asyncio event loop, its MCP dispatch loop, or FastMCP session manager is actually servicing requests -- only that something is willing to accept a socket connection. A backend whose event loop is deadlocked (e.g. blocked in a synchronous call, or genuinely stuck waiting on a CDP/Chrome response with no path to yield) can still complete a bare TCP handshake, so all three consumers of this function report 'healthy'/'running' throughout the entire incident.
why_it_matters: This is the root cause of the whole incident being invisible: `stealth-chrome-devtools status`/`doctor` will print 'backend: running on port 19222' even while every tool call through it hangs forever, the automatic liveness watchdog (the only self-healing mechanism in the codebase) never arms because it polls this same weak signal, and the eviction logic that would let a fresh, correctly-versioned backend take over is gated behind '_find_running_server() == port' returning non-None -- which it will, for a wedged-but-socket-open backend. The one integration test that exercises recovery (tests/test_proxy_backend_death.py) proves this gap by its own design: its docstring and test body explicitly cover the backend being killed (hard death, socket goes unhealthy), never a backend that stays alive but stops answering -- the exact incident scenario given.
fix_direction: Replace/augment the liveness probe with an application-level check: send a lightweight real MCP request (or a dedicated /healthz endpoint answered by the FastMCP dispatch loop itself, analogous to the existing _await_backend_http startup probe) on an interval, and use THAT signal for status/doctor, the liveness watchdog, and the eviction guard -- not a bare socket connect.
adv_rebuttal: The hardest attack (is there ANY application-level liveness probe?) fails: _await_backend_http (singleton.py:316) DOES perform a real initialize->200, but it is called only at startup (460), never for liveness; _watch_backend_liveness (388) and _find_running_server (134) both use the raw-TCP _server_is_healthy (83-89). A wedged event loop still completes the kernel TCP handshake, so all three consumers read 'healthy'. test_proxy_backend_death.py only covers hard kill (191,205) and injected-unhealthy checks, never wedged-but-listening. Critical stands.

There is no stop, restart, or kill-orphans verb. The one function that can actually evict a running backend, singleton._clear_stale_backend(), is never called from the CLI at all -- it only runs internally, mid-startup, inside _start_backend_holding_lock() when a NEW backend is already being spawned. Likewise, ProcessCleanup._recover_orphaned_processes() (the well-implemented, command-line-scoped, create_time-gated orphan-Chrome killer) only runs inside ProcessCleanup.__init__(), i.e. only as a side effect of a fresh backend process starting -- there is no way to invoke it standalone.
why_it_matters: Combined with F-301 (a wedged same-version backend is reported 'reusable' forever), this means an on-call engineer who has correctly diagnosed a hung backend still has NO supported command to fix it. The only path is: manually discover the PID (see F-305 -- also unsupported), manually taskkill/terminate it outside any tooling the repo provides, and only THEN does the next session's ensure_server_running() spawn a fresh backend, whose ProcessCleanup.__init__() will (safely, by user-data-dir + create_time matching) clean up the orphaned Chrome as a side effect. Every step of actual recovery is manual and undocumented; the repo automates the safe-matching logic but not the trigger.
fix_direction: Add `stealth-chrome-devtools stop`/`restart` (read the pid from server.json, terminate() with a wait/kill escalation like _kill_process_by_pid already does, then optionally respawn) and `stealth-chrome-devtools kill-orphans` (invoke ProcessCleanup's recovery pass standalone, without requiring a full backend restart).
adv_rebuttal: Confirmed: _DISPATCH (cli.py:219-225) exposes only status/profiles/cleanup/doctor/serve; _clear_stale_backend is never called from the CLI (only mid-startup in _start_backend_holding_lock, singleton.py:290), and _recover_orphaned_processes runs only in ProcessCleanup.__init__ (process_cleanup.py:55). No stop/restart/kill-orphans verb exists. The enterprise 'on-call/MTTR' framing is inflated for a single-user local tool where the operator has OS-level process control, but the capability gap itself is real and correctly described.

what: The detached HTTP backend -- the single process that does all the real work once more than one Claude Code session is active (per singleton.py's own module docstring, this is the normal/expected mode, not an edge case) -- has stdout and stderr wired to os.devnull at the moment it is spawned. Every print(), every uncaught-exception traceback, every uvicorn access/error log line, and every debug_logger._emit_stderr() call (even when STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG=true) from that process is silently discarded. Grepping the entire tracked source tree confirms there is no FileHandler, RotatingFileHandler, or logging.basicConfig anywhere -- the only logger in the codebase (embedded/debug_logger.py) is an in-memory Python object (plain lists on a DebugLogger instance), disabled by default (self._enabled = False), with no disk persistence unless a caller explicitly invokes export_to_file / export_to_file_paginated.
why_it_matters: There is no log file to open at 3am. Not 'hard to find' -- it structurally cannot exist for the process that matters, because its output streams are wired to the null device before a single line is ever written. If the wedged/crashed backend is killed (per F-302's manual recovery) or crashes on its own, every diagnostic signal that existed only in that process's memory (errors, warnings, info, the stack trace of whatever actually deadlocked it) is gone forever, unrecoverable by any means -- not rotated-and-archived-elsewhere, just never captured.
fix_direction: Attach a real log sink to the detached backend: a stdlib logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler under a documented path (e.g. STATE_DIR/logs/backend.log), written unconditionally at INFO+ regardless of STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG, instead of DEVNULL. Keep DEVNULL only for stdin.
adv_rebuttal: Facts airtight: _start_server_process wires stdout/stderr/stdin to DEVNULL unconditionally (singleton.py:229-231) on the sole detached-backend spawn path, and a repo-wide grep finds zero FileHandler/RotatingFileHandler/basicConfig/getLogger — no file logging exists (can't be killed). But the IDENTICAL fact is rated High as F-503, so the finding-set contradicts itself; 'no persistent backend logs' is an observability gap (the tool still works after restart), not a functional defect, and Critical rests on a production-outage narrative overstated for a single-user local tool. Reconcile to High to match F-503.

This 15-line CLI section is the entire operational documentation for the project. It lists the 5 commands and one line of what each prints; it never discusses failure modes, what 'backend not running' vs 'backend running but unresponsive' means, where any state lives on disk, or what to do when something is stuck.
why_it_matters: This is the direct, verifiable answer to 'do the docs tell you where to look first': no. Every other finding in this report (F-301 through F-305, F-307, F-308) is a gap that written operator documentation could partially compensate for even without a code fix -- and none of that documentation exists. A 3am on-call engineer's only source of truth is the source code itself, read cold, under time pressure.
fix_direction: Add a TROUBLESHOOTING.md covering: where state lives (~/.stealth-mcp/), how to distinguish a dead backend from a wedged one (until F-301 is fixed, explicitly document the current blind spot), how to safely find and stop the backend process today (manual PID lookup via server.json + taskkill/kill, until F-302 exists), and when automatic orphan-Chrome recovery does and does not trigger.

Real docs-completeness gap, low impact.

what: log_error/log_warning/log_info (debug_logger.py:62-153) each do `with self._lock:` (an untimed threading.Lock acquire) and call this _emit_stderr from inside that lock scope. print() to a pipe is a blocking syscall when the consumer isn't draining it -- it does not raise; it just blocks. The except clause here only catches (OSError, ValueError), which covers a closed/broken pipe, not a slow/full one. If any thread ever blocks inside this print while holding self._lock, every other thread's next log_error/log_warning/log_info call hangs forever on the untimed `with self._lock:` -- with no asyncio.wait_for or timeout anywhere in the codebase able to rescue it, since it is a synchronous blocking primitive, not an awaitable.
why_it_matters: This is a code-level hang with no possible timeout backstop -- directly answering 'what would have surfaced this hang' with 'nothing could have, by construction.' It requires STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG=true (or DEBUG=true) and a real, undrained stderr pipe to trigger -- true for a manually-run `serve` in a terminal/script but not for the singleton-spawned production path, which DEVNULLs stderr (F-303) and therefore can't backpressure. Note clear_debug_view() (line 257) and export_to_file_paginated() (line 336) already use the safer `self._lock.acquire(timeout=5.0)` pattern with a lock-free fallback -- the three logging methods are the odd ones out.
fix_direction: Match the timeout+fallback pattern clear_debug_view already uses: `self._lock.acquire(timeout=...)`, and/or move the _emit_stderr call outside the lock scope entirely (stderr output doesn't need the same mutual exclusion as the shared list mutation).
adv_rebuttal: Code confirmed: log_error/_warning/_info hold an untimed `with self._lock:` and call _emit_stderr inside it (lock-before-emit ordering verified), whose print(...,stderr) only excepts (OSError, ValueError) at L57-59 - a full/undrained pipe blocks without raising, deadlocking all logging. But it requires STEALTH_BROWSER_DEBUG=true AND a real undrained stderr pipe; the production singleton path DEVNULLs stderr and cannot backpressure, so it is largely unreachable. Real latent hang plus a real inconsistency with the timed-lock siblings (clear_debug_view L257), but the narrow reachability caps it below High.

But for a small tool published by a single vendor, the absence of a documented contribution/release workflow is normal and low-impact; the 'ship a one-line change' onboarding framing is inflated for a single-maintainer, single-user context. Real gap, Low severity.

what: Ongoing backend liveness is judged solely by opening a TCP connection to the port. `_watch_backend_liveness` (line 368/388) defaults to this probe, resets its failure counter on any successful connect, and only tears the proxy down after 3 consecutive *connection* failures. A backend whose event loop / FastMCP session manager is wedged (but whose uvicorn listen socket is still open in the kernel) passes this probe forever. The stronger real-`initialize`->HTTP-200 probe (`_await_backend_http`, line 316) — whose own docstring warns 'a freshly bound uvicorn socket can answer (4xx) while FastMCP's MCP session manager is still starting' — is used ONLY once at startup, never for liveness.
why_it_matters: Every session is a thin stdio proxy to ONE shared backend, so a single wedged backend hangs all sessions at once. Because the liveness signal cannot distinguish 'listening' from 'able to serve MCP', the proxy never tears down, the client never reconnects, and tool calls hang until the 120s BACKEND_READY_TIMEOUT and then indefinitely on later calls. This is the exact mechanism that turns one stuck request into a total, self-perpetuating outage that no automatic recovery clears.
fix_direction: Use the real MCP readiness probe (`_await_backend_http`, or a lightweight tools/list round-trip) inside `_watch_backend_liveness`, not a bare TCP connect, so a wedged-but-listening backend is detected and torn down for reconnect.
adv_rebuttal: Same mechanism as F-301, verified independently: _watch_backend_liveness (singleton.py:368-398) defaults to the TCP _server_is_healthy (388) and only tears down after 3 consecutive CONNECT failures, while the real-initialize probe _await_backend_http (316) is used once at startup (460) and never for liveness — its own docstring even warns a bound socket can answer before MCP is ready. A wedged uvicorn loop still accepts the socket, so the monitor never arms. No test covers wedged-but-listening (test_proxy_backend_death.py uses backend.kill). Critical stands.

quote:
When multiple Claude Code sessions start simultaneously, this module ensures
only ONE HTTP server process is spawned. All sessions connect to it as
lightweight stdio proxies.
what: The whole design centres on a single per-user backend process (STATE_DIR = ~/.stealth-mcp, one server.json, one port). When any new session sees a version mismatch, `_start_backend_holding_lock` calls `_clear_stale_backend`, which terminates/kills the backend process squatting the port (singleton.py:200-208: `proc.terminate()` ... `proc.kill()`) so its own fresh backend can bind.
why_it_matters: One shared backend is a single point of failure for every concurrent session, and the eviction path makes it worse: a routine `uvx`/`pip` upgrade (or two projects pinned to different versions) means a newly-started session terminates the backend that other, still-running sessions are actively proxying to. Their in-flight browser automation — including browser subprocesses parented by that backend — dies mid-operation. A per-session action (starting a new client) thus causes a cross-session outage.
fix_direction: Reference-count or per-session-isolate the backend, or refuse to evict a backend that has live sessions; at minimum key the shared backend on both version AND a liveness lease so an upgrade spins up a second backend instead of killing the one in use.
adv_rebuttal: Real: one per-user backend, and _clear_stale_backend terminates/kills it (L202/L206) from _start_backend_holding_lock (L290). But eviction fires ONLY on a version mismatch (L132), so a same-version new session does NOT evict a peer - the 'starting a new client causes a cross-session outage' phrasing overstates it. The single-backend SPOF is an intentional, reasonable tradeoff for a local single-user tool, and the cross-session kill needs concurrent sessions at divergent versions (an upgrade race), which is occasional rather than routine. Severity inflated.

what: The singleton backend — the process that runs ALL browser automation for ALL sessions — is spawned fully detached with stdout, stderr and stdin routed to DEVNULL. There is no log file, no supervisor, no auto-restart, and (DETACHED_PROCESS / start_new_session) it is not a child of any session that could observe it.
why_it_matters: When the backend wedges (F-501) or dies (F-502) there is zero diagnostic output to explain why, and nothing restarts it. Recovery is entirely manual and blind: an operator must know a hidden backend exists, discover port 19222, identify the orphaned PID, and kill it by hand — while every session stays down. This is the pillar that turns any backend incident into a long, high-MTTR outage.
fix_direction: Redirect backend stdout/stderr to a rotating log under STATE_DIR, add a health-based supervisor that restarts a dead/wedged backend, and surface backend pid/port/log location via the ops CLI (`stealth-chrome-devtools doctor`).
adv_rebuttal: Confirmed: _start_server_process (singleton.py:218-241) spawns with DETACHED_PROCESS/CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP (or start_new_session) and DEVNULL on all three streams; no supervisor exists, and no file log is written (grep: zero FileHandler/basicConfig). One nuance worth recording: on backend DEATH there IS a de-facto restart (the liveness watchdog tears down -> client reconnects -> fresh backend spawns), so 'no auto-restart' strictly holds only for the wedge case; but the detached/DEVNULL/no-log pillar and manual wedge-recovery are all correct.

what: Backend reuse identity is the installed package version STRING. `_find_running_server` (line 132) reuses a running backend whenever `state.get("version") == _server_version()`. The version string does not change when the source in `embedded/server.py` is edited in place (editable/dev install stays '1.2.0'), so after editing code and reconnecting the client, `ensure_server_running` finds the still-running same-version backend and transparently proxies to the OLD code.
why_it_matters: Edits silently do not take effect. A developer changes a tool, reconnects, observes the old behaviour, and concludes the fix is wrong — chasing a phantom. The project's own saved memory ('server.py edits need a fresh backend process, not just a reconnect') exists because this already burns people. Combined with F-503 (the backend is an invisible detached process) and F-506 (hot_reload can't reload server.py), every change requires manually hunting and killing an orphaned process that nothing tells you about. This is the primary velocity sink behind the missed deadline.
fix_direction: Gate reuse on the actual code identity (hash/mtime of the installed package files) rather than the version string, and/or honour a STEALTH_MCP_FRESH_BACKEND=1 dev env var that forces eviction+respawn on connect.
adv_rebuttal: Same as F-206, confirmed at HEAD: _find_running_server reuses on the version STRING (singleton.py:132) from importlib.metadata (263-269); an in-place edit to embedded/server.py leaves '1.2.0' unchanged, so ensure_server_running proxies to the old-code backend. The branch's version-aware work fixes CROSS-version reuse, not this same-version-edit case, and the project's own saved memory documents this exact burn. Nothing invalidates on source change.

what: `embedded/server.py` is a 4207-line god module holding 96 `@section_tool` tool bodies plus profile-clone, storage-sweep, and lifecycle logic, all behind a custom section_tool/DISABLED_SECTIONS indirection layer over FastMCP. Nearly every behavioural change lands in this one file.
why_it_matters: A 4000+ line single-file surface makes any change slow to locate, hard to review, and wide in blast radius; the custom registration/disable layer adds a second thing to reason about. It is the structural reason estimates for 'small' tool changes balloon, and the reason regressions are easy to introduce and hard to isolate.
fix_direction: Split the god file into per-section tool modules that register against a shared FastMCP instance; keep server.py as thin composition/wiring only.
adv_rebuttal: Factually correct - same 4208-line / 96-tool god module as F-101 (verified), with the section_tool/DISABLED_SECTIONS indirection layer over FastMCP. It holds as a maintainability finding, but it is a DUPLICATE of F-101 (and F-612): three High findings for one root cause. Recommend main merge them so the issue is counted once.

quote:
modules_to_reload = [
            'browser_manager',
            'network_interceptor',
            'dom_handler',
            'debug_logger',
            'models'
        ]
what: The built-in `hot_reload` tool advertises 'Hot reload all modules without restarting the server' but omits `server.py` itself (where the 96 tool bodies live), so tool-code edits are never reloaded. For the modules it does reload it calls `importlib.reload` then re-instantiates managers via reassigned module globals (2996-2997: `global browser_manager, BrowserManager` / `browser_manager = BrowserManager()`).
why_it_matters: It gives a false sense that live reload is supported, deepening the F-504 phantom-debugging trap. Worse, swapping `browser_manager` for a fresh instance orphans every already-open browser held by the old manager (split-brain: existing sessions unreachable, processes leaked), so invoking it mid-session can itself corrupt state or strand browsers.
fix_direction: Either remove hot_reload (rely on a real backend respawn) or make it correctly reload the tool modules AND migrate/close live state instead of dropping references to running managers.

quote:
- name: Run unit tests
        run: uv run pytest -m "not integration" -v --tb=short
what: The PyPI publish job (`publish` needs `test`) runs ONLY `pytest -m "not integration"` before shipping. The real-browser integration suite is never a release gate. The coverage floor in test.yml is likewise computed with `-m "not integration" ... --cov-fail-under=39`, so the entire live-automation surface (browser spawn, CDP, navigation, cloning — most of the 4207-line god file) is excluded from the measured coverage by construction.
why_it_matters: A release can be green and still be broken in every real browser path, because the code that actually drives Chrome is never exercised before publish and contributes nothing to the 39% floor. Nothing protects the highest-risk, hardest-to-test surface; regressions ship to users and are discovered only in the field.
fix_direction: Make the integration job a required predecessor of `publish` (or run at least a browser smoke test in the release workflow); track integration coverage separately so the live surface can't silently stay at zero.
adv_rebuttal: Two facts are true - publish.yml's only gate is a non-integration `test` job (needs: test L37; run L32) and the coverage floor is computed on non-integration at --cov-fail-under=39. But the central impact claim ('nothing protects the browser surface; regressions ship, found only in the field') is wrong: test.yml has a dedicated integration-tests job that installs real Chrome + Xvfb (L57-62) and runs `pytest -m integration` on every branch/PR (L74), per its own comment that feature branches must get 'full unit + integration CI.' Browser paths ARE gated pre-tag, so publish not re-running them is redundant, not a hole. Impact exaggerated (and the finding was self-marked unverified: paraphrased, 0.8, verified=false).

why_it_matters: A single semantic change to how an element is captured (a new CSS edge case, a selector fix, an asset rule) must be made and re-verified in up to five places. Divergence between the variants is inevitable, and the duplicated surface multiplies the cost and risk of any cloning change — a direct contributor to schedule overrun.
fix_direction: Collapse the five cloners onto one core extraction engine with thin output adapters (in-memory vs file vs progressive/CDP), so behaviour is defined once.

quote:
def _start_server_process(port: int):
    cmd = [
        sys.executable,
        "-m",
        "stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp",
        "--transport", "http",
        "--port", str(port),
        "--host", "127.0.0.1",
    ]
what: The backend is always started on the fixed DEFAULT_PORT = 19222 with no fallback. `_backend_pid_on_port`'s docstring states 'A foreign process holding the port is deliberately ignored (never returned for termination)', so if any unrelated process holds 19222, `_clear_stale_backend` cannot free it, the fresh backend fails to bind and dies (silently — DEVNULL, F-503), and there is no attempt to pick another port.
why_it_matters: A port collision on a single hard-coded port is an unrecoverable, invisible outage: the proxy answers `initialize` locally so the client looks connected, but every real tool call waits out BACKEND_READY_TIMEOUT (120s) and then fails, with no log to explain it. The project already has a `_free_port()` helper in proxy_forwarder.py, so the fixed port is an avoidable brittleness.
fix_direction: Bind to an OS-assigned free port and publish it via server.json (already the discovery mechanism), or fall back to another port on bind failure; never depend on one hard-coded port being free.

from debug_logger import debug_logger

why_it_matters: A bug fix or CDP/DOM API change to element-extraction logic (styles, events, animations, CSS rules) has to be manually replicated across up to 3 independently-implemented extractors; nothing enforces they stay in sync, so tool behavior silently drifts depending on which cloner module backs a given MCP tool.
fix_direction: Consolidate the CDP/JS extraction primitives into element_cloner.py and have comprehensive_element_cloner.py and cdp_element_cloner.py delegate to it the way file_based_element_cloner.py already does; delete whichever whole-file implementation(s) turn out to be unused by any registered tool.

Real duplication - but essentially a DUPLICATE of F-203 (and overlaps F-140); recommend main merge F-203/F-601 into one.

what: server.py resolves ~12 separate environment variables via direct os.getenv() calls scattered across the file (lines 55, 62, 226, 235, 242, 249, 257, 984, 1004-1006, 4088), each with its own inline default/fallback branch. Only 2 of these (parse_bool_env/parse_float_env) route through a shared helper; the rest — including all of the path-resolution functions shown above — repeat the same 'getenv or fall back' shape ad hoc with no central config schema or single source of truth.
why_it_matters: Every new environment-configurable setting requires writing another one-off getenv+fallback function instead of extending a single config surface, and there's no single place to see or validate all runtime configuration — a new contributor has to grep the whole file to find every env var the server actually reads.
fix_direction: Introduce a single config module/dataclass that resolves all STEALTH_MCP_*/BROWSER_* environment variables once at a defined point, with the individual _default_session_root/_master_profile_dir/etc. helpers reading from it instead of calling os.getenv directly.

what: The `_with_cdp_timeout` wrapper is invoked 59 times in server.py, and the identical 'tab = await browser_manager.get_tab(instance_id); if not tab: ... Instance not found' preamble appears 49 times — nearly every one of the 96 @section_tool functions repeats this same instance-lookup/timeout-wrap boilerplate verbatim instead of sharing it through a decorator or helper.
why_it_matters: Any change to how instance lookup or CDP timeout errors are reported (e.g., adding structured error codes, or fixing a bug in the not-found path) has to be replicated by hand across ~50-59 call sites in a 4200-line file; it's easy for new tools to copy-paste an outdated variant of the preamble.
fix_direction: Extract the instance-lookup + _with_cdp_timeout pattern into a decorator or context manager that tool functions apply once, collapsing the repeated 3-5 line preamble into a single line per tool.

quote:
project_root = Path(__file__).parent.parent
sys.path.append(str(project_root))
what: file_based_element_cloner.py anchors its sys.path addition two levels up (Path(__file__).parent.parent, i.e. the stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp/ package root), while comprehensive_element_cloner.py (line 23) anchors one level up (Path(__file__).parent, i.e. embedded/ itself) for what is conceptually the same 'make sibling embedded modules importable' operation.
why_it_matters: The two anchors put different directories on sys.path. file_based_element_cloner.py's parent.parent puts stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp/ itself on sys.path as a bare-import root — and that directory also contains a *different* top-level server.py (src/stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp/server.py) alongside embedded/server.py, so a bare `import server` elsewhere in the codebase (e.g. cli.py:37) can resolve to whichever one was imported first depending on module import order.
fix_direction: Standardize both files on the same anchor (embedded/, matching what the entrypoint already puts on sys.path via cli.py's _ensure_embedded_on_path), and prefer the package-relative try/except import file_based_element_cloner.py already uses elsewhere in the same file.

quote:
network_response = NetworkResponse(
                request_id=request_id,
                status=response.status,
                headers=dict(response.headers) if hasattr(response, "headers") else {},
                content_type=response.mime_type if hasattr(response, "mime_type") else None,
                body=body,
            )
            async with self._lock:
                self._responses[request_id] = network_response
what: NetworkInterceptor.__init__ declares `self._requests`/`self._responses` as plain dicts (network_interceptor.py:21-22, no maxlen/LRU). `_on_response` fetches the *full* response body via CDP `get_response_body` (line 159) and stores it unconditionally on every captured response, with no per-body size cap and no total-store size cap; entries only disappear when `clear_instance_data` runs the pop/del cleanup for that instance_id (lines 601-605), i.e. on instance close.
why_it_matters: A long-running browser session that loads many pages or makes heavy XHR/fetch traffic accumulates every response body in memory for the life of the instance; on network-heavy workloads this is a realistic path to hundreds of MB of resident memory and eventual OOM of the whole MCP server process, taking down every other active instance with it.
fix_direction: Cap stored body size per response (truncate/drop bodies over a threshold) and/or cap total store size with LRU eviction, independent of instance close.
adv_rebuttal: Confirmed and strengthened: _responses is a plain dict (network_interceptor.py:22); _on_response fetches the full body (159) and stores it unconditionally (182) with no per-body or total cap, cleared only on instance close. Crucially _on_response applies NO include/exclude filter (only _on_request does, 108-115), so even set_capture_filters (advertised 'to reduce memory usage', server.py:2241) cannot bound response-body memory. Interception is set up unconditionally on every spawn_browser (server.py:1396-1399) — default-on — and unbounded body accumulation matches the tool's own heavy-scraping workload.

what: `matches()` is the per-request hook-matching check; every network event that reaches a hook with a `custom_condition` re-parses and re-evaluates the condition source string from scratch via bare `eval()`. No compiled-code-object caching (e.g. `compile(..., 'eval')` stored on the hook instance) exists anywhere in the file.
why_it_matters: On pages generating heavy network traffic with custom-condition hooks active, the repeated parse+compile cost of eval() is paid on every single request/response event instead of once per hook lifetime, adding avoidable per-event CPU overhead.
fix_direction: Compile the condition source once when the hook is registered (compile(condition_code, '<hook>', 'eval')) and eval() the cached code object on each match instead of the raw string.

what: `_file_lock` attempts a non-blocking lock (LK_NBLCK / LOCK_NB) and, if that raises OSError/IOError because another process already holds it, the `except` branch still `yield`s — the caller (`_load_tracked_pids`/`_save_tracked_pids`) proceeds to read/write the PID-tracking file exactly as if the lock had been acquired, with no signal that it wasn't. Load and save are also two separate top-level calls, so a load-modify-save sequence by one process is not atomic with respect to another process doing the same.
why_it_matters: Under real contention (e.g. two server instances racing to record/clean up tracked browser PIDs at the same time), one process can silently read a half-written file or overwrite the other's concurrent update, corrupting or losing PID-tracking metadata used for orphan cleanup — and there is no error or log signal that this happened, so the failure is invisible.
fix_direction: Make lock-acquisition failure visible to the caller (return/raise instead of silently yielding), and/or make load-modify-save a single locked critical section instead of two independently-locked calls.

what: In the non-recovery branch of `_kill_processes_for_metadata` (normal explicit close, not startup orphan recovery), the stored `fallback_pid` is used directly with zero verification when the profile-dir PID lookup comes up empty. Compare the `if recovery:` branch immediately above (lines 371-410), which calls `psutil.Process(pid).create_time()` and checks it against both `self._init_time` and the stored `create_time` before trusting a PID.
why_it_matters: If the OS has recycled the stored PID for an unrelated process by the time close/cleanup runs, this path kills that unrelated process with no identity check at all — the exact class of bug the recovery branch was specifically hardened against, just left unfixed on this branch.
fix_direction: Apply the same create_time/identity check used in the recovery branch to the non-recovery fallback_pid path before killing.

quote:
def clear_debug_view_safe(self):
        """
        Safe version that recreates data structures if lock fails.
        """
        try:
            self.clear_debug_view()
        except Exception:
            self._errors = []
            self._warnings = []
            self._info = []
            self._stats = defaultdict(int)
            self._emit_stderr("[DEBUG] Debug logs force-cleared (lock bypass)")
what: clear_debug_view_safe's except branch reassigns self._errors/_warnings/_info/_stats to brand-new objects without holding self._lock, unlike every other mutation site in the file (log_error/log_warning/log_info and clear_debug_view itself all wrap mutations in `with self._lock:`).
why_it_matters: If this force-clear path runs concurrently with another thread mid-append under the lock (e.g. `with self._lock: self._errors.append(...)`), the reassignment can race with that append and silently drop the in-flight log entry — low-impact since it's the debug-log subsystem, but a real unsynchronized-write bug.
fix_direction: Reassign the buffers inside `with self._lock:` (using a short blocking acquire) instead of unconditionally in the except branch, or drop the lock-bypass fallback entirely.

what: The `hot_reload` tool reloads browser_manager/network_interceptor/dom_handler modules and then replaces the module-global singleton instances with brand-new, empty instances (`browser_manager = BrowserManager()`, etc.) rather than migrating or preserving the existing tracked instance/session state.
why_it_matters: Calling hot_reload while any browser instance is open orphans it: the new BrowserManager() has no record of instances the old one was tracking, so those browsers become unreachable through the API even though the underlying Chrome processes keep running — a direct resource leak and a surprising, easily-triggered operability failure for a tool whose whole purpose is 'reload without restarting'.
fix_direction: Either block/warn hot_reload when instances are active, or migrate existing tracked state (self._instances, etc.) from the old manager object into the newly constructed one instead of discarding it.
adv_rebuttal: Confirmed: hot_reload executes `browser_manager = BrowserManager()` (server.py:2997), a fresh empty instance whose _instances dict does not carry the old singleton's tracked browsers, so any open instance is orphaned (get_instance/navigate/close_instance all miss it). With BROWSER_IDLE_TIMEOUT defaulting to '0' (server.py:222), the abandoned old idle reaper won't auto-close them either, so the real Chrome processes leak — exactly the failure mode for a tool whose stated purpose is 'reload without restarting'.

what: list_instances() (browser_manager.py:588-603) does prune instances whose OS process has died, via `_browser_process_is_alive`. That check is purely process-level (subprocess.poll() / psutil is_running()) — it never performs an actual CDP protocol round-trip (e.g. a lightweight Runtime.evaluate) to confirm the DevTools connection itself is responsive.
why_it_matters: A browser whose OS process is alive but whose CDP/websocket connection has died (crashed renderer, dropped socket, hung tab) passes this check and is reported by list_instances as a healthy 'active' instance; callers only discover it's actually unusable when a subsequent tool call against it times out via _with_cdp_timeout.
fix_direction: Add a cheap CDP-level liveness probe (e.g. a short-timeout Runtime.evaluate or Target.getTargetInfo) to list_instances/health-check paths, in addition to the existing process-liveness check.

what: server.py is a single 4207-line module (verified via `wc -l`) containing nearly all 96 MCP tool definitions, config resolution, timeout/validation helpers, session/profile management, and orchestration logic for every other embedded/ subsystem.
why_it_matters: A single 4000+ line file with 96 tool functions is hard to navigate, review, or safely change in isolation — every PR touching server.py risks unrelated merge conflicts and every reviewer has to hold the whole file's context to reason about a single tool's correctness; this is the file every other cross-cutting finding in this audit (F-602, F-603, F-605 area, etc.) lives inside.
fix_direction: Split by @section_tool section (browser-management, element-extraction, cdp-functions, debugging, etc.) into separate modules re-exported from a thin server.py, now that the coverage gate (P-022/P-023, already fixed) provides a safety net for the refactor.
adv_rebuttal: Factually correct (4208 lines, ~96 tool definitions, verified) and holds as a maintainability finding. But it is a DUPLICATE of F-101 and F-505 - the same god-object root cause stated a third time, adding no distinct claim. Recommend main collapse F-101/F-505/F-612 into a single finding.

what: file_based_element_cloner.py hedges its debug_logger import with a try/except relative-then-bare fallback, then immediately also does a raw sys.path.append() two lines later. comprehensive_element_cloner.py, doing conceptually the same job, skips the try/except entirely and only does the bare sys.path.append + bare import (`from debug_logger import debug_logger`, no relative-import fallback) — see F-604/comprehensive_element_cloner.py:23-26.
why_it_matters: Two different, inconsistent strategies for making embedded/ modules importable are live in the same directory; whichever one a given module picked determines whether it degrades gracefully under a real package install (where relative imports work) versus the sys.path-mutation execution mode. This fragility is exactly what the god-module split (F-612/P-024) would need to resolve cleanly rather than propagate into new files.
fix_direction: Standardize all embedded/ modules on one import strategy (prefer package-relative imports, matching how the installed package is actually structured) and drop the sys.path mutation once the entrypoint reliably puts embedded/ on sys.path once, up front.

quote:
"""Singleton server management for multi-session environments.

When multiple Claude Code sessions start simultaneously, this module ensures
only ONE HTTP server process is spawned. All sessions connect to it as
lightweight stdio proxies.

Race condition handling:
  - File lock ensures exactly one process starts the server
  - Losers of the lock race poll until the server is healthy
  - Exponential backoff prevents thundering herd on health checks
  - Fallback to standalone stdio mode if server fails to start
"""
what: The module is named and documented as covering only the "singleton" dedup guarantee (one backend process across concurrent sessions), but the 571-line file actually implements five distinct subsystems under that one name: file-lock singleton election (_exclusive_lock, _start_backend_holding_lock), version-aware backend discovery/eviction (_find_running_server, _clear_stale_backend, _is_our_backend), an HTTP readiness prober (_await_backend_http), a full bidirectional stdio-to-streamable-HTTP proxy bridge (_proxy_streams, ~150 lines handling session-id capture, initialize-swallowing, task groups), and a standalone liveness watchdog (_watch_backend_liveness). None of that breadth is signaled by the name "singleton.py" or its docstring.
why_it_matters: Every Stage-2 fix plan reviewed so far routes through this one file by line number (M1 liveness at singleton.py:83, M2 reuse-key at singleton.py:132,263, M3 observability touches the same proxy code, M7/M8 recovery work is adjacent). An agent asked to "add a correlation id to backend requests" or "fix the stdio proxy's reconnect logic" has no naming signal that this is the file to open — "singleton" reads as pure dedup bookkeeping, not the transport layer. The mismatch actively misroutes exactly the kind of edits this audit's own roadmap prioritizes next.
fix_direction: Split by responsibility into modules whose names match their content, e.g. backend_lifecycle.py (election/discovery/eviction/version-gate) and stdio_proxy.py (the streams bridge + watchdog). disposition: defer:known-debt (singleton.py split backend_lifecycle/stdio_proxy -> wave 4 post-M2; sequence-critical, four plans edit this file serially)

async def get_tab(
        self,
        instance_id: str,
        touch_activity: bool = True,
    ) -> Optional[Tab]:
        """
        Get the main tab for a browser instance.

        Args:
            instance_id (str): The ID of the browser instance.
            touch_activity (bool): Whether retrieving the tab should refresh last activity.

        Returns:
            Optional[Tab]: The main tab if found, else None.
        """
what: `get_tab`/`get_browser` are the two most-called accessors in the codebase — nearly every one of the 96 tools opens with `tab = await browser_manager.get_tab(instance_id)`. Both take a `touch_activity: bool = True` parameter that, on by default, updates the instance's last-activity timestamp used by the idle-timeout reaper (`touch_instance`) as a side effect of every call. The name "get_tab" promises a pure read; by default it also writes. No @section_tool-wrapped tool in server.py ever passes `touch_activity` explicitly — every call site is `browser_manager.get_tab(instance_id)` with no second argument — so this mutation is invisible not just from the tool name but from every layer an AI model would actually read; the exposed tool docstrings (e.g. execute_script's) never mention that calling the tool resets an idle timer.
why_it_matters: An agent debugging "why didn't this idle instance get reaped", or reasoning about the side effects of a call that reads like read-only (get_page_content, get_element_state, etc. all route through get_tab), has no way to discover this from the name or the exposed docstring — it would need to trace into browser_manager.py internals, which the clarity bar for this lens explicitly says shouldn't be necessary.
fix_direction: Either flip the default so the name matches behavior (`touch_activity: bool = False`, with call sites that need liveness extension opting in explicitly), or rename to signal the write (e.g. a wrapper `get_tab_and_touch` used by tool call sites, leaving a true `get_tab` read-only). At minimum, surface "refreshes the instance's idle timer" in every public tool docstring that transitively calls it.
disposition: route:M7 (flip touch_activity default or rename to signal the write)

what: self._lock (a plain threading.Lock(), :40) is held two structurally different ways in the same class with no stated rule for which to use: (a) bare `with self._lock:` -- unbounded blocking, no owner/timestamp bookkeeping -- at log_error (:75), log_warning (:114), log_info (:141), get_debug_view_paginated (:183); (b) manual `self._lock.acquire(timeout=5.0)` + try/finally `self._lock.release()` + `self._lock_owner`/`self._lock_acquired_time` bookkeeping -- at clear_debug_view (:268) and export_to_file_paginated (:363-381, quoted above). The drift is not just cosmetic: export_to_file_paginated acquires the lock via style (b) at :363, then -- still holding it -- calls self.get_debug_view_paginated(...) at :373, whose body re-enters the *same* lock via style (a)'s bare `with self._lock:` at :183. threading.Lock is not reentrant, so this nested acquire is made by the thread that already holds the lock: the bounded 5s timeout at :363 only guards the outer acquire, the inner one inside get_debug_view_paginated has no timeout at all and blocks forever, every time export_to_file_paginated is called.
why_it_matters: export_debug_logs / export_to_file_paginated is a tool an operator reaches for specifically when something is already wrong -- per the audit's own 3am-incident trace, this is exactly the kind of tool that needs to be trustworthy under stress. A structural self-deadlock on that path (mechanically distinct from the already-logged, lower-confidence F-307 pipe-blocking scenario -- this one needs no DEBUG flag and no full stderr pipe, just a normal call, since it is pure same-thread Lock reentrancy) means the one tool meant to help diagnose a stuck server can itself hang. This is a code-inspection finding, not an executed repro, hence confidence below 1.0, but the reentrancy mechanics follow directly from documented threading.Lock semantics.
fix_direction: Pick one discipline for the whole class -- either always bounded acquire(timeout=...) with try/finally, or switch self._lock to threading.RLock() so nested same-thread acquisition is safe by construction (the smaller, safer fix given the existing call graph).
disposition: AMEND:plan_M3-step-M3-4 Amendment A1 (RLock + re-entrancy pinning test) — human-approved 2026-07-02
