what: The codebase has two incompatible idioms for 'wait up to N seconds for something to happen'. Idiom A: `await asyncio.wait_for(coro, timeout=t)` wraps a single coroutine -- the dominant pattern, e.g. the shared _with_cdp_timeout/_with_timeout helper (embedded/server.py:149: `return await asyncio.wait_for(coro, timeout=t)`), and direct call sites in browser_manager.py (:654,668,760,946,956,1008,1022,1026), cdp_function_executor.py (:696), proxy_forwarder.py (:161,200,218,240,252,398). Idiom B: a hand-rolled `deadline = time.monotonic() + N` loop with time.sleep/asyncio.sleep between checks -- used exclusively in singleton.py for backend-process lifecycle: _wait_for_server (:248-256, sync, exponential backoff, quoted above), _await_backend_http (:316-358, async, fixed 0.1s interval), and the port-release wait inside _stop_existing_backend (:211-215). Neither idiom is wrong in isolation -- B is arguably necessary since these poll a condition rather than await one coroutine -- but there is no shared, named helper for 'poll until healthy or timeout', so singleton.py has three separately hand-rolled copies of the same loop shape instead of one.
why_it_matters: A future contributor adding a new 'wait for X to become ready' need (the audit's own change-trace scenario) has two live examples to copy from and no rule for which is canonical for which situation, so the inconsistency compounds with each addition -- the same cost the audit measured directly for the missing 'write artifact to disk' precedent. This is independent of the already-tracked plan_M1 dedup debt (consolidating _backend_http_ready's copied body into _await_backend_http): even after that consolidation lands, the resulting single probe body is still idiom B, still a second wait-strategy relative to asyncio.wait_for used everywhere else.
fix_direction: Extract one `await poll_until(check, timeout, interval=0.1, backoff=1.0)` helper and have all three singleton.py deadline-loops call it; keep asyncio.wait_for as the canonical idiom for bounding a single coroutine. Document the distinction (bound-one-call vs poll-a-condition) once so it is a choice, not an accident.
disposition: route:M2 (opportunistic poll_until() ONLY if M2 touches those loops; else known debt)

what: The exact same env-parsing body is independently defined twice under the identical name _parse_nonnegative_int_env: as a module-level function in browser_manager.py:31-56 (3-arg signature with a `minimum` parameter) and again as a @staticmethod inside ProcessCleanup in process_cleanup.py:57-78 (2-arg signature, minimum hardcoded to the literal 0 at line 76's `if parsed < 0:`). Neither file imports the other's copy. Separately, server.py:54-67 defines two more siblings of the same 'typed env var with fallback' family, parse_bool_env and parse_float_env, which browser_manager.py and process_cleanup.py also never import despite both files reading several of their own env vars with the same getenv-or-fallback shape.
why_it_matters: Four independent implementations of one concept ('parse a typed environment variable with a safe fallback') exist across 3 files with no shared home. The two _parse_nonnegative_int_env copies already show the earliest sign of drift: browser_manager.py's version accepts a caller-supplied minimum, process_cleanup.py's silently assumes 0, so a future call site in process_cleanup.py that needs a different floor has no way to express it without copy-pasting a third time or reaching into browser_manager.py's private helper.
fix_direction: Move parse_bool_env/parse_float_env (server.py) and _parse_nonnegative_int_env (browser_manager.py's more general 3-arg version) into one small shared module (e.g. embedded/env_utils.py) and have all three call sites import from it; delete process_cleanup.py's private copy entirely.
disposition: route:M11a (one env-parsing home env_utils.py)

what: response_stage_hooks.py's ResponseStageProcessor class (execute_response_action, lines 22-78; execute_request_action, lines 80-144) independently re-implements the entire block/fulfill/redirect/modify CDP Fetch-domain dispatch that DynamicHookSystem._execute_hook_action (dynamic_hook_system.py:435-519) already performs for both request and response stages via its own request.stage branching. response_stage_hooks.py:148 builds `response_stage_processor = ResponseStageProcessor(None)  # Will be set by dynamic_hook_system`, but dynamic_hook_system.py never imports ResponseStageProcessor, response_stage_processor, or the response_stage_hooks module at all -- the wiring the comment promises never happened, so this second 148-line implementation is dead code, reachable only from its own dedicated test file (tests/test_response_stage_hooks.py).
why_it_matters: This duplication has already rotted, which is the concrete cost of not sharing one implementation: dynamic_hook_system.py's fulfill branch base64-encodes the response body before calling fulfill_request (dynamic_hook_system.py:453-455/461, `body_base64 = base64.b64encode(body_bytes).decode('ascii')` ... `body=body_base64`) because CDP's Fetch.fulfillRequest requires a base64 body, but response_stage_hooks.py's copy of the same branch (response_stage_hooks.py:42-47) skips that step and passes `body=action.body or ""` raw. If this dead class were ever wired in as its own comment promises, it would emit malformed response bodies. This is a materially bigger and different-shaped instance than the existing header-loop finding against dynamic_hook_system.py: a second, currently-unreachable file-and-class-level copy of the whole dispatch method (of which the repeated header-to-HeaderEntry loop at response_stage_hooks.py:38-40/53-55/101-104/117-119 is only one ingredient), not one more repetition of a 4-line loop inside the same method.
fix_direction: Delete response_stage_hooks.py and tests/test_response_stage_hooks.py. If response-stage-specific dispatch is genuinely still wanted, express it as a parameter/branch on the one live _execute_hook_action instead of a hand-maintained parallel class, so there is exactly one place that knows how to turn a HookAction into CDP Fetch-domain calls.
disposition: route:M12a (DELETE dead response_stage_hooks.py ResponseStageProcessor + its test)

what: singleton.py owns the only importable definitions of DEFAULT_PORT (=19222) and STATE_DIR (Path.home()/'.stealth-mcp'), but neither is imported anywhere else in src/. The port literal 19222 is re-typed independently in src/stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp/server.py:23 (`parser.add_argument("--singleton-port", type=int, default=19222)`) and in cli.py:258 (`serve.add_argument("--port", type=int, default=19222, help="HTTP port (default 19222)")`, where the literal appears twice on the one line). The state-dir root is re-typed in response_handler.py:25 (`return Path.home() / ".stealth-mcp" / "element_clones"`, whose own docstring at lines 18-20 says it deliberately 'mirrors the project's existing state-dir convention' rather than importing it) and, divergently, in embedded/server.py's _default_session_root (embedded/server.py:225-231), which uses a same-level sibling name `".stealth-mcp-browser-sessions"` on POSIX and an entirely different, non-home-relative root `C:\stealth-mcp-browser-sessions` on Windows instead of nesting under STATE_DIR.
why_it_matters: Two 'define once, consume everywhere' constants instead ended up with 2-3 independently re-typed copies apiece, and the directory one has already visibly drifted: response_handler.py at least kept the same `.stealth-mcp` root by hand-copying the string, but embedded/server.py's session root broke off into a same-purpose but differently-named and differently-rooted directory. Any future change to the port default or the state-dir root (e.g. a per-OS relocation) silently misses whichever of these sites the editor doesn't know to check, and on Windows two of this server's own data directories (`~/.stealth-mcp` and `C:\stealth-mcp-browser-sessions`) don't even share a common parent to discover or clean up together.
fix_direction: Export DEFAULT_PORT and STATE_DIR from singleton.py as the single source of truth; have cli.py's and server.py's argparse defaults import DEFAULT_PORT, and have response_handler.py and embedded/server.py's _default_session_root build their paths as a subdirectory of the imported STATE_DIR (e.g. STATE_DIR / "browser-sessions") instead of re-typing the root.
disposition: route:M15 (export DEFAULT_PORT/STATE_DIR; other sites import)

what: cli.py is a second, independently-shipped entry point (`stealth-chrome-devtools`, pyproject.toml:48 `[project.scripts]`, distinct from the MCP server's own `stealth-chrome-devtools-mcp` entry) whose entire command surface is implemented by reaching into leading-underscore ('private') functions of two collaborator modules it doesn't own — there is no public API to use instead. Across its 4 read/mutate subcommands, cli.py calls 15 distinct private symbols on `server` (the bare-imported embedded/server.py, via `import server` at cli.py:37) across 22 call sites: `_clone_root_dir`, `_clone_storage_cap_bytes`, `_session_storage_cap_bytes`, `_idle_autoclones_over_cap`, `_named_profiles_over_session_cap`, `_dir_size_bytes`, `_regenerable_size`, `_enforce_clone_storage_cap_in`, `_enforce_named_profile_trim_in`, `_clone_is_auto` (cli.py:52), `_clone_is_named` (cli.py:54), `_profile_has_running_browser` (cli.py:69), `_master_profile_dir` (cli.py:72), `_master_snapshot_dir` (cli.py:73), `_default_session_root` (cli.py:100,171) — plus 2 private symbols on `singleton` (embedded/singleton.py) across 3 sites: `_find_running_server` (cli.py:99,173), `_server_version` (cli.py:102). Not one non-underscore function of either module is ever called from cli.py. This is the same disease F-109 named in server.py itself (3 instances reaching into process_cleanup/debug_logger, rated Low) but roughly 6x larger in instance count and located in a wholly separate file the original finding never examined — server.py's and singleton.py's 'private' surface is, in practice, cli.py's only public API.
why_it_matters: This lens asks whether a module can be understood, and safely changed, by reading only itself plus its imports' signatures. Here the answer fails from both directions: reading embedded/server.py or singleton.py alone, nothing marks these ~17 underscore-prefixed functions as a load-bearing external contract — Python's own naming convention says the opposite. Reading cli.py alone, `server`/`singleton` are bare `import` targets with no type stub or `__all__`, so there is no way to tell which of their symbols are stable. A maintainer (or agent) decomposing server.py per the already-approved M4 plan, and renaming an internal helper with no visible in-file caller, would silently break the `stealth-chrome-devtools` CLI. There is a real safety net today — tests/test_cli.py exercises `_cmd_status`/`_cmd_profiles`/`_cmd_cleanup` end-to-end and would catch most breaks — but that net only fires if someone runs the full suite after touching a 'private' helper in an unrelated-looking file; the design gives no signal that they need to.
fix_direction: Give `server` and `singleton` a small explicit public surface for the handful of things cli.py actually needs (session-root/profile inventory + size/role, storage-cap read + enforce, backend liveness + version query) and repoint cli.py's ~25 call sites at it; alternatively, if these were always meant to be shared internals rather than private, drop the leading underscore on just that subset and document them as the intentional cross-file contract they already are. Do this before/alongside M4's server.py decomposition so the split module boundaries preserve whatever cli.py depends on.
disposition: route:M4-Ph1 (public CLI-facing surface at registry formalization; extends F-109)

what: embedded/ (20 .py files, ~13,600 lines) is never consumed as a Python package anywhere in this codebase — embedded/__init__.py is empty, and every one of its modules imports its siblings with bare names (`from debug_logger import debug_logger`, `from browser_manager import BrowserManager`, etc.). An import-graph scan of all intra-embedded import statements found 0 using a real `from .sibling import X` relative form as their primary path. This only works because three independent call sites mutate `sys.path` before any embedded module is first imported: the package's own entrypoint shim (quoted above, server.py:11-18), cli.py:23-26's separate copy of the identical `sys.path.insert(0, ...)` logic, and tests/conftest.py's own copy (comment: 'Make embedded/ importable the same way the real entrypoint does'). Two leaf modules additionally hedge with their own redundant, mutually-inconsistent `sys.path.append` (comprehensive_element_cloner.py:24, file_based_element_cloner.py:16 — already flagged by F-604/F-613 for disagreeing with each other on anchor depth and import-fallback style). This finding is the broader root cause those two symptoms sit on top of: even if F-604/F-613's inconsistency were fixed so the two files agreed, the package would still not be a real package — no file inside embedded/ can be import-checked or opened by an agent and understood as self-resolving without also knowing which of 3 external call sites ran first.
why_it_matters: This is close to a literal instantiation of this lens's own named test: opening any single file in embedded/ — say network_interceptor.py — its own sibling imports cannot be resolved by reading that file plus 'its imports' signatures,' because the import target isn't reachable at all until an unrelated file (server.py, cli.py, or conftest.py, whichever happens to run first in a given process) has already mutated global interpreter state. It also creates a latent double-import hazard: every module in embedded/ is registered in sys.modules under its bare name (browser_manager, singleton, ...), never under stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp.embedded.browser_manager. Nothing prevents a future test, script, or agent-written module from doing the ordinary Pythonic thing — `from stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp.embedded.browser_manager import BrowserManager` — which would either fail outright or, worse, succeed and construct a second, distinct BrowserManager class incompatible with (fails isinstance against) the one every existing singleton and test actually uses. Today this is avoided only because all ~27 bare-import call sites in tests/ and every production code path happen to agree on the convention — an agreement that exists nowhere in writing.
fix_direction: Make embedded/ a real subpackage: convert its intra-package imports to `from . import X` / `from .x import Y` throughout (mechanical, and breaking changes are explicitly free on this branch), then delete the now-redundant sys.path.insert/append calls in server.py, cli.py, conftest.py, and the two leaf cloners. If the standalone-script execution mode (`runpy.run_path(..., run_name="__main__")` in server.py:34) must be preserved, keep exactly one sys.path-setup helper (e.g. in embedded/__init__.py) that every entrypoint calls, instead of three independently-maintained copies.
disposition: route:M4-Ph1 STEP 0 (make embedded/ a real package; mechanical import rewrite after singleton-churn plans land)

what: BrowserManager (browser_manager.py:59, 1336 lines, the largest embedded module after server.py) is a single class whose ~30 methods span at least six unrelated concerns, already visible in its own constructor: spawn/teardown lifecycle (spawn_browser line 311, close_instance line 605, close_all line 1327), an idle-watchdog subsystem with its own timer task (start_idle_reaper/stop_idle_reaper/_run_idle_reaper lines 269-235, touch_instance line 219, the _idle_reaper_task/_idle_timeout_seconds_default state quoted above), navigation (navigate line 961, get_navigation_tab/_replace_main_tab/_wait_for_navigation_condition lines 872-960), tab CRUD (list_tabs/switch_to_tab/get_active_tab/close_tab/get_tab lines 1104-1210), instance/page-state derivation (get_instance/list_instances/update_instance_state/get_page_state/get_spawn_diagnostics lines 571-1296), and proxy-forwarder lifecycle (_close_proxy_forwarder line 193, the _proxy_forwarders dict quoted above). This is the same disease as M4 (server.py) one layer down, and distinct from M13's finding about spawn_browser specifically: M13's fix (splitting the 236-line method into a pipeline of small private methods) leaves every one of those small methods on this same class, so the class would still mix all six concerns after M13 lands.
why_it_matters: A change to idle-reaping behavior requires reading and reasoning about a 1336-line file that also defines navigation-retry logic, tab-switching, and proxy-forwarder teardown — none of which the change touches, but all of which share the same self._instances/self._lock state and could be affected by a careless edit. A limited-context agent asked to 'add an idle-timeout override' cannot scope its reading to 'the idle-watchdog part of BrowserManager' because there is no such boundary in the file; it must load the whole class to be safe about lock/state interactions with the other five concerns.
fix_direction: Extract the idle-watchdog (a ReaperTask/IdleReaper collaborator holding _idle_reaper_task + its loop) and the navigation helpers (a Navigator collaborator wrapping navigate/_replace_main_tab/_wait_for_navigation_condition) out of BrowserManager into their own modules, leaving BrowserManager as a thinner instance registry/facade that composes them. Sequence after M6's characterization tests and alongside M13, since both touch the same class.
disposition: defer:known-debt (BrowserManager 6-concern split -> M4-Ph2 era; record in DESIGN.md at M14)

what: DebugLogger (debug_logger.py:15 — the module the audit's own change-trace found imported across 16 files / up to 287 call sites) mixes two unrelated jobs under one name: being an in-memory ring-buffer logger (log_error/log_warning/log_info lines 62-128, get_debug_view*/clear_debug_view* lines 157-294) and being a four-format file-serialization engine (export_to_file/export_to_file_paginated lines 324-343 quoted above, dispatching to _export_lockfree line 405, _export_gzip_pickle line 450, _export_pickle line 466, _export_json line 483). The export machinery is roughly 150 of the file's 499 lines — about a third of the module, inside a class whose name promises only 'logging.'
why_it_matters: Because debug_logger is the single most widely-imported internal module in the codebase, every one of those 16 importing files pulls in gzip/pickle serialization machinery it never calls just to get a logger. An agent opening this file to answer 'how does logging work here' — the question its name invites — has to read past four export code paths first. Splitting it would also make each half readable in isolation for what it actually is: the export half depends on filesystem paths and format negotiation that have nothing to do with the logging half's job of recording events; today a reader can't tell where one job's concerns end and the other's begin because they share one class and one file.
fix_direction: Move export_to_file, export_to_file_paginated, and the four _export_* format backends into a separate debug_log_exporter.py (or similar) that takes a debug-view snapshot as input; DebugLogger keeps only recording/retrieval (log_*, get_debug_view*, enable/disable). Small and mechanical — does not touch the 287 call sites that only ever call log_*.
disposition: defer:known-debt (DebugLogger serialization engine -> wave-4 cleanup; record in DESIGN.md at M14)
